generated_abstract: The reliability and efficiency of aircraft maintenance are critical factors in the aviation industry, with significant implications for safety, cost-effectiveness, and operational performance. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) have sparked interest in leveraging its capabilities to revolutionize various aspects of aircraft maintenance, including predictive maintenance, fault diagnosis, and health monitoring. This study undertakes a comprehensive examination of AI applications in aircraft maintenance through a systematic literature review. A thorough search of academic publications yielded a dataset of 696 papers spanning four decades (1984-2024), documenting the integration of AI technologies such as Automated Reasoning and Deep Learning with airworthiness management, health monitoring, and maintenance operations. The analysis identifies key AI technologies, associated aircraft maintenance processes, emerging trends, prominent research venues, and underexplored areas. Ultimately, this review provides insights into the adoption of AI in aircraft maintenance, highlighting potential implications for researchers, practitioners, educators, and other stakeholders seeking to optimize maintenance practices through innovative AI-based solutions.

This study examines the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in online discussion forums to support autonomous learners. As AI becomes increasingly prevalent in education, it is essential to investigate its impact on the learning experience. A systematic review was conducted using the SALSA framework and PRISMA flow diagram to identify 12 articles that employed AI in discussion forums. The extracted articles covered six applications of AI, including intelligent tutoring systems, chatbots, and automatic formative assessment. Three types of man-machine partnerships were observed: student-AI, student-AI-student, and student-AI-teacher. Analysis revealed that cognitive presence was more predominant than social presence, with a focus on individual learning achievements. Three perspectives emerged regarding the role of AI in learning: supplementing human teaching functions, facilitating information collection, and providing automatic evaluation and feedback. The study also highlighted the importance of communication in acquiring knowledge through AI-mediated interactions. The findings demonstrate that AI is utilized in various forms, including chatbots and generative AI, to support student learning and facilitate connections between students and teachers. Overall, this research contributes to our understanding of the complex interplay between technology, pedagogy, and the learning environment, known as entangled pedagogies.

This study aims to transform traditional reactive maintenance strategies in infrastructure and facilities management into proactive, data-driven approaches leveraging advancements in artificial intelligence. Conventional maintenance methods often rely on fixed schedules or post-failure interventions, which are inadequate for mitigating unexpected downtimes and escalating costs. To address these limitations, this research applies multiple machine learning algorithms to a comprehensive synthetic predictive maintenance dataset that encapsulates key operational metrics such as temperature, torque, rotational speed, and tool wear across diverse failure modes.

The objective of this study is to evaluate the performance of various machine learning models in predicting multiclass failures and identify the most effective approach. The comparative analysis reveals that Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) substantially outperforms alternative models, achieving a remarkable accuracy of 98.9% in multiclass failure prediction, supported by an Area Under the Curve (AUC) nearing 1.0 and F1 scores above 0.98 in both validation and test sets.

The Random Forest Classifier (RFC) and Support Vector Classifier (SVC) closely follow, each delivering precision and recall rates exceeding 95%. Notably, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) provides rapid inference, facilitating real-time applications despite slightly lower accuracy metrics (~96.6%). Advanced preprocessing techniques, such as feature scaling, label encoding, and synthetic minority oversampling (SMOTE), enhance model robustness amid inherent class imbalances.

The findings underscore the potential of AI to revolutionize maintenance by offering granular failure diagnostics and enabling timely interventions, thus significantly reducing operational costs and preventing catastrophic infrastructure failures. This research advances the state-of-the-art by integrating interpretability, cross-model benchmarking, and practical scalability considerations, positioning AI-driven predictive maintenance as a cornerstone of modern infrastructure resilience and sustainability.

Machine learning (ML) methods have shown promise in detecting depression from social media posts; however, their effectiveness in Arabic-speaking populations remains understudied. This research addresses this knowledge gap by introducing the ArabMindGuard (AMG) dataset, comprising 3083 expertly annotated tweets from Saudi Arabia, and systematically comparing ML performance across five datasets representing multiple Arabic dialects, including Saudi, Jordanian, and Modern Standard Arabic (MSA). A rigorous evaluation framework was employed, incorporating precision, recall, F1-score, and statistical significance testing to assess the performance of nine ML classifiers. Advanced Arabic-specific natural language processing techniques were leveraged, including Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF), N-gram analysis, and CAMeL tools, to evaluate the efficacy of ensemble methods and Support Vector Machines (SVM). The results indicate that ensemble methods and SVM consistently outperform other approaches, achieving F1-scores ranging from 0.82 to 0.95 across the datasets. These findings highlight the significance of dialectal variation in depression detection and provide foundational baseline metrics for future deep learning applications. This study contributes to culturally responsive digital mental health interventions in the Arab world by establishing methodological benchmarks for Arabic mental health text analysis.

The integrity and transparency of electoral processes are increasingly dependent on the reliable administration of voting systems, particularly in large-scale elections where timely declaration of results is crucial. However, existing security methods often suffer from limitations such as single points of failure (SPOF) and poor scalability. Moreover, blockchain-based solutions that rely on Proof of Work (PoW) or Proof of Stake (PoS) introduce their own challenges, including reduced throughput, high energy consumption, and vulnerability to emerging post-quantum attacks. Recent instances of electoral misinformation campaigns have highlighted the potential for chaos and compromised voter privacy in contemporary systems. To address these concerns, this study proposes a novel blockchain-based hybrid consensus voting mechanism called Post Quantum Secured Hierarchical Authoritative Consensus (PQSHAC). PQSHAC incorporates an optimized sharding protocol that leverages the distributed nature of election data to reduce skewness and increase shard cohesion. Additionally, a token generation protocol is employed to prevent extraneous voting and ensure voter privacy. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of PQSHAC in achieving high throughput and robust security against quantum computing attacks via Dilithium-3 integration. Notably, the proposed modular ledger architecture reduces storage requirements by approximately 60% compared to non-modular approaches. The findings of this study underscore the strengths of PQSHAC as a secure and efficient solution for improving trust and dependability in contemporary voting systems.

This study addresses a critical issue in Continuously Reinforced Concrete Pavement (CRCP) infrastructure management, namely transverse cracking, which compromises ride quality, service life, and maintenance strategies. To improve the accuracy of transverse crack prediction, a novel hybrid framework is proposed that integrates Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) with Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM). A comprehensive dataset comprising 395 records from 33 pavement sections in the Long-Term Pavement Performance (LTPP) program was utilized to inform model development. The PSO algorithm was employed to optimize critical GBM hyperparameters, including iteration count, learning rate, and tree depth. Results indicate that the optimized PSO-GBM model exhibits superior performance compared to benchmark models such as conventional GBM, Random Forest, Support Vector Regression (SVR), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Linear Regression, with an average Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 1.62 and a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.99 under 5-fold cross-validation. Sensitivity analysis revealed that pavement layer thicknesses (L3 and L4) and Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT) are the most influential factors in transverse crack development, consistent with theoretical expectations. The robustness and stability of the proposed approach were validated through residual distribution plots and equality line analyses across various severity levels.

The proliferation of misinformation in digital media poses a pressing concern that necessitates effective solutions. Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a promising approach for detecting genuine and fake news. This study develops an enhanced convolutional neural network (CNN) model to improve the accuracy of news authenticity recognition. The proposed CNN model leverages deep learning techniques, which are optimized through iterative refinement. Experimental results demonstrate that the improved CNN model significantly outperforms its counterparts in identifying authentic and fabricated content. Specifically, after 50 iterations, it achieves an impressive accuracy rate of 96.97%, surpassing a baseline model based on random deactivation techniques by 7.29%. The model's superiority is also evident across various datasets, including FakeNewsNet and LIAR. On the former dataset, its normalized mutual information reaches 84.82%, representing a notable improvement over traditional methods (5.05% increase) and models based on random inactivation techniques (10.25% increase). Similarly, on the latter dataset, it achieves an adjusted Rand index of 87.32%. The study's primary contribution lies in harnessing AI technology to develop robust CNN models for mitigating the spread of misinformation, thereby safeguarding societal information security, upholding public right-to-know principles, and promoting the dissemination of accurate news.

In the realm of intelligent manufacturing, the accurate classification of weld bead defects is a critical challenge in Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) processes, particularly in robotic welding applications. To address this issue, we propose a novel hybrid machine learning approach that synergizes the strengths of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Vision Transformers (ViT). This innovative framework leverages CNN's ability to extract localized features with high precision, while ViT provides global contextual awareness, enabling the model to capture complex relationships between weld bead characteristics. We demonstrate that our hybrid architecture outperforms traditional architectures such as ResNet and conventional CNN models in terms of classification accuracy. The performance evaluation of our proposed model reveals its robustness and reliability across diverse operational scenarios, underscoring the potential of integrating hybrid deep learning models into industrial automation systems to enhance weld bead defect detection. By reducing operational inefficiencies and ensuring consistent weld quality, our findings contribute significantly to the advancement of manufacturing process efficiency in GMAW applications.

This study proposes an innovative hybrid framework for sentiment analysis specifically designed for the Greek language. By integrating a novel lexicon construction methodology with Active Learning (AL) strategies, we aim to improve performance and reduce annotation effort. A domain-specific lexicon was developed using two custom metrics: Score<inf>w</inf>, which captures the sentiment polarity of words, and Weight<inf>w</inf>, which reflects their combined emotional and statistical importance. These metrics enabled the transformation of tweet-level annotations into semantically rich word-level representations. To evaluate the framework's efficacy, we trained four classification models (Naïve Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, and Decision Trees) with and without lexicon-derived features. The results demonstrate significant improvements in accuracy and F1-score when incorporating the lexicon, particularly for Logistic Regression and Decision Tree models. Furthermore, a dynamic Active Learning approach was employed, where the lexicon was reconstructed in each cycle using the evolving labeled dataset. This strategy achieved comparable levels of accuracy and Macro F1-score to full-supervision, while utilizing only 30% of the data. The findings suggest that combining lexicon enhancement with uncertainty-based sampling offers a cost-effective and scalable solution for sentiment analysis in low-resource languages such as Greek.

In materials design, machine learning predictors derived from word embeddings of atoms have garnered significant attention in recent years. However, the reliability and robustness of these predictors are contingent upon the variability inherent in their underlying representations. This study investigates whether the static word embeddings affiliated with physicochemical entities exhibit variability due to information heterogeneity within training material. Specifically, we examine the effects of vocabulary term selection on individual atomic embeddings and assess their impact on domain-specialized information transfer. Our analysis reveals substantial variability in atomic embeddings, which is largely influenced by the choice of language modelling terms. Notably, this variability does not preclude the successful mapping of materials' composite predictors into physicochemical properties when joined atomic embeddings are incorporated within a regression model estimating compound stability through formation energy prediction. Furthermore, our results demonstrate that vector calibration via dimensional reduction maintains the encoded information and predictive performance stability. This research provides empirical evidence on the magnitude of variability in word embeddings of physicochemical entities, highlighting their potential to facilitate stable predictive performance despite origin-dependent differences.

The development of machine learning models for atomic configurations has given rise to numerous sets of descriptors, yet many of these are plagued by incompleteness or functional dependence. Incomplete descriptor sets fail to accurately capture the complexities of atomic environments, while complete constructions often suffer from redundancy, where certain descriptors can be expressed as functions of others. This redundancy does not contribute to improved discrimination between atomic configurations and instead increases computational complexity.

To address this issue, we employ pattern recognition techniques to identify and remove dependent descriptors, resulting in a minimal set that satisfies completeness. We apply this strategy in two distinct contexts: the refinement of an existing description, the atomic cluster expansion, and the augmentation of an incomplete construction. The latter yields a novel message-passing neural network architecture capable of recognizing up to 5-body patterns.

Our results demonstrate the efficacy of this approach in optimizing descriptor sets across various descriptors and application datasets. Notably, the proposed architecture exhibits strong accuracy on state-of-the-art benchmarks while maintaining low computational costs. These findings underscore the importance of carefully selecting and refining descriptor sets to enhance machine learning model performance.

Silicon carbide (SiC), a technologically relevant material, exhibits unique properties in high-energy grain boundaries (HEGBs). However, the diffusion mechanism of cesium (Cs) within these HEGBs remains poorly understood due to limitations in computational modeling. Specifically, long-time scale simulations are hindered by the lack of reliable and computationally efficient interatomic potentials. This study addresses this challenge by developing a machine learning framework for constructing moment tensor potentials (MTPs), which enables efficient active training on complex structures. Our approach facilitates the extraction of unknown regions from HEGBs, allowing for the first time simulations of Cs diffusion in both amorphous SiC and HEGBs. The results indicate that Cs diffuses via a cage-breaking mechanism, supported by radial distribution functions, Voronoi analysis, and electronic structure calculations. These findings provide valuable insights into local atomic environments and weak interactions governing Cs mobility. Furthermore, this work presents a generalizable workflow for training machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) on complex structures, offering a significant advancement in modeling fission product behaviors.

The ionospheric Spread-F phenomenon has garnered substantial attention in both ionospheric electrodynamic research and radio-based technology applications, including communication and navigation systems. However, the classification of Spread-F remains largely dependent on manual interpretation of ionograms by experts, which is plagued by inefficiencies (~10 seconds per figure) and subjective biases. Currently, there is a lack of publicly available ionogram datasets that classify Frequency/Range/Mix/Strong Range SF (FSF/RSF/MSF/SSF) through either human labor or machine processing.

To address this limitation, we present an expert-guided ionogram dataset that represents the most comprehensive collection in terms of class coverage, the largest volume, and the most extensive temporal span to date. This dataset comprises 150,000 ionograms (30,000 per class, including a "non-SF" group) spanning 14 years from 2002 to 2016, thereby capturing a diverse range of solar and geomagnetic conditions. Based on this dataset, we develop a classification model using a deep learning architecture that can be applied to further data, facilitating more efficient and objective classification of ionograms. This study aims to bridge the gap between manual interpretation and automated processing in ionospheric research, ultimately contributing to enhanced operational applications in radio-based technologies.

The accuracy of current survival prediction models for extra-nodal natural killer/T-cell lymphoma (ENKTL) is suboptimal, necessitating the development of more effective predictive tools. This study aimed to address this limitation by designing and validating a machine learning-based algorithm that integrates clinical and pathological covariates to predict patient outcomes. A comprehensive dataset comprising 977 subjects from four cohorts was analyzed to evaluate model performance using a range of metrics, including Harrell's c-index, receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, calibration curves, and decision curve analysis (DCA). The results indicate that the Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) outperformed other machine learning algorithms tested in this study, leading to the development of the ENKTL-ML score. The performance of the ENKTL-ML score was evaluated across multiple cohorts, yielding c-index values ranging from 0.82 to 0.84, indicating a high degree of accuracy. Furthermore, comparison with established models (IPI, KPI, PINK-E, and NRI) revealed that the ENKTL-ML score significantly outperformed these benchmarks in predicting survival outcomes. This study demonstrates the potential of machine learning-based approaches in improving the prognostic accuracy for patients with ENKTL, facilitating more informed clinical decision-making.

The proliferation of electric vehicles has sparked a pressing need for accurate estimation of remaining driving range, particularly in cargo transportation applications. This study addresses this challenge by developing a machine learning-based approach to predict the remaining driving range of cargo electric vehicles (CEVs). A comprehensive dataset was generated through real-world testing of a Musoshi Pop-Up Mini electric cargo vehicle on a 2 km campus route under varying load and speed conditions, allowing for high-resolution modeling of energy consumption patterns. The dataset underwent systematic preprocessing, including feature engineering and segment-wise aggregation, to prepare it for analysis. Seven regression algorithms – ElasticNet, Support Vector Regression, Random Forest, LightGBM, XGBoost, CatBoost, and ExtraTrees – were evaluated using Optuna-based Bayesian hyperparameter tuning and compared based on their performance metrics (RMSE, MAE, R²). Among the tested models, the Support Vector Regression (SVR) model yielded the most accurate predictions, with an RMSE of 2.37, MAE of 1.75, and R² of 0.892. These results demonstrate that data-driven approaches can effectively estimate energy consumption and driving range for CEVs, thereby facilitating safer and more reliable deployment of electric mobility systems.

The proliferation of e-commerce has dramatically altered consumer behavior, necessitating the development of sophisticated predictive models for digital marketing strategies. This study investigates the potential of integrating machine learning (ML) models with Structural Equation Modelling (SEM) to forecast consumer purchase intentions. A comprehensive dataset comprising consumer reviews and behavioral signals from prominent e-commerce platforms is employed to examine the efficacy of various ML algorithms, including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Neural Networks, in conjunction with sentiment analysis and TF-IDF text vectorization. Furthermore, a SEM framework is proposed and validated to link consumer trust, product quality perception, and sentiment polarity to buying intentions. The results demonstrate that ensemble ML models outperform linear classifiers and provide theoretical grounding by validating latent constructs. Notably, the hybrid SEM-ML framework achieves both predictive accuracy and theoretical rigor. This research offers practical insights for e-commerce managers seeking to optimize customer engagement strategies. To enhance theoretical clarity and robustness, the SEM is anchored within established theories, including the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) and Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB), with testable hypotheses specified. Sentiment analysis is conducted using both VADER and DistilBERT models, and validated on a manually annotated subsample. Time-aware validation and a robustness check are also performed to ensure the reliability of the findings.

Here is a rewritten abstract based on the source text:

The accurate identification and diagnosis of mental disorders and learning disabilities are crucial for timely intervention, yet traditional assessments often fall short in terms of efficiency and effectiveness. Recent advances in machine learning and feature selection techniques hold promise for improving these outcomes, but their application to large transdiagnostic datasets and quick assessment tools remains underexplored. This study addresses this gap by applying machine learning and feature selection methods to a comprehensive dataset featuring over 1,000 questionnaire items from the Healthy Brain Network (n = 4,136). A two-stage feature selection approach was employed to identify optimal item subsets for assessing various disorders and symptoms, as well as custom outcome measures for learning disabilities. The performance of these models was then compared to existing assessments through rigorous cross-validation procedures. Our results demonstrate that machine learning models using parsimonious item subsets significantly outperform traditional assessments (p = 0.004), with AUC values reaching up to 0.855 for specific learning disorders. Notably, restricting analysis to non-proprietary assessment items did not compromise performance. This study showcases the potential of leveraging existing datasets to develop efficient and effective assessment tools for mental health professionals, highlighting the importance of open-source software architectures that facilitate adaptation to diverse datasets while emphasizing the need for external validation prior to clinical implementation.

The increasing prevalence of myopia poses a significant global health challenge, necessitating innovative approaches to its management. To address this concern, we developed the Myopia Progression Predictive Model (MPPM), an artificial intelligence framework based on the Transformer architecture. This model comprises two primary components: the Natural Progression Module (NPM) and the Intervention Progression Module (IPM). NPM was trained on a comprehensive dataset of refractive records from over 304,000 children and adolescents, resulting in high predictive accuracy for spherical equivalent (SE) and axial length (AL) progression over a decade. Specifically, internal testing revealed strong correlations between predicted and actual values, with SE predictions achieving an R² value of 0.94 and mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.35 diopters, while AL predictions reached R² = 0.91 and MAE = 0.16 millimeters. The model's performance was also validated in external cohorts. Building on this foundation, IPM was designed to forecast the effects of specific interventions, including atropine therapy, orthokeratology, peripheral defocus spectacles, and repeated low-level red light (RLRL) therapy. By leveraging a causal machine learning framework, MPPM enables individualized predictions of treatment outcomes, with SE and AL predictions demonstrating high accuracy across all interventions. Notably, RLRL therapy was found to slightly reverse myopia progression, while the other interventions slowed its advancement. The MPPM model holds considerable promise as an AI-driven platform for optimizing pediatric myopia management, offering a personalized approach to predicting and mitigating the effects of this prevalent condition.

The production of nanofibers through electrospinning is a complex process influenced by multiple parameters, including process conditions, environmental factors, and solution properties. As a result, achieving optimal nanofiber quality requires extensive experimentation and fine-tuning. However, the scarcity of publicly available datasets hinders modeling efforts and limits the establishment of reference parameters for stable nanofiber formation. To address this challenge, we have developed Cogni-e-Spin DB 1.0, a comprehensive dataset comprising 809 experimental records of electrospinning parameters and corresponding nanofiber morphologies. This dataset is designed to provide a large-scale, diverse, and machine-learning-ready resource for researchers seeking to understand the complex relationships between process conditions and nanofiber properties. Our dataset enables thorough analyses of process-structure-property interactions and supports the application of machine learning methods to gain insights into electrospinning phenomena. To validate the dataset's reliability and utility, we have verified its integrity against original publications and demonstrated its effectiveness through proof-of-concept machine learning modeling. The Cogni-e-Spin DB 1.0 dataset is intended to accelerate fundamental research, model development, and process optimization in various electrospinning applications, while a companion web platform facilitates live contributions and interactive exploration of the data, promoting continuous community-driven updates.

Recent advancements in machine learning have revolutionized the field of cancer cell classification, with a growing emphasis on leveraging high-resolution microscopy images to extract informative features. However, traditional methods often rely heavily on manual preprocessing and handcrafted feature extraction, which can introduce user bias and limit scalability. This study addresses these limitations by developing an automated, interpretable, and organelle-focused deep learning framework for classifying breast cancer cell lines from fluorescence microscopy images.

The proposed framework employs a novel end-to-end approach that incorporates patch-based sampling, sparsity filtering, and channel-wise intermediate fusion to extract and integrate organelle-specific features. To ensure model interpretability, Grad-CAM visualizations and single-organelle classifier analyses were employed to examine the relationships between different organelles and classification decisions.

The framework was evaluated on a dataset comprising fluorescence microscopy images from six breast cancer cell lines using 5-fold cross-validation, yielding a classification accuracy of 97.1±1.1%. Notably, this performance surpassed or matched conventional handcrafted feature-based approaches while eliminating the need for manual segmentation and 3D rendering steps. Further analysis revealed that mitochondria played a crucial role in classification decisions, underscoring the importance of organelle morphology and spatial organization.

The proposed framework offers a scalable, automated, and interpretable deep learning solution that has significant implications for microscopy-based phenotyping and computational pathology. By leveraging organelle-specific features, this study demonstrates the potential for improved cancer cell classification accuracy and sheds light on the underlying mechanisms driving classification decisions.

The incidence of accidental falls among individuals aged 65 and older has become a pressing public health concern due to its high frequency and severe consequences. Such falls can lead to fractures, traumatic brain injuries, and long-term complications if timely intervention is not provided. In response, researchers have been developing automated fall detection systems that integrate intelligent algorithms with sensor-based data acquisition to facilitate rapid response and medical assistance. This study undertakes a systematic literature review within the PRISMA framework to examine the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) approaches in fall detection. A comprehensive search across major databases yielded 182 relevant articles after removing duplicates and applying strict inclusion/exclusion criteria from an initial pool of 596 articles. The review encompasses a wide range of AI/ML techniques applied to fall detection using diverse sensor modalities, including wearable, vision-based, ambient, and multimodal systems. Furthermore, it summarizes the publicly available datasets and sensor configurations used in existing studies. This comprehensive perspective enables comparison of trade-offs across sensing modalities and informs effective system development for real-world deployment. The review provides a comparative overview of AI/ML-based fall detection approaches, highlighting differences in accuracy, sensitivity, and dataset usage as reported in the literature. Moreover, it outlines several open research challenges in using AI and ML to detect falls and presents potential future perspectives for advancing this field.

Retinitis pigmentosa (RP) is a hereditary retinal degeneration characterized by night blindness, progressive loss of peripheral vision, and eventual blindness. Current treatments, including gene therapy and retinal prosthetics, are limited in their applicability due to the complexity and heterogeneity of RP. To address this challenge, we developed a diagnostic and prognostic model for RP utilizing large-scale deep learning (DL) architectures pre-trained on fundus image datasets. Our results indicate that the EfficientNetB4 model achieves an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) of 0.94 in diagnosing RP, with improved performance in patients with preserved vision. For visual prognosis, we employed machine learning survival analysis to integrate DL-derived image features and clinical metadata, adopting a strict patient-level split to mitigate data leakage. Notably, our hybrid model combining imaging and clinical data outperformed models relying on either modality alone, particularly among female patients. Time-dependent AUC analysis revealed that prognostic performance peaked between 500 and 1400 days post-examination. SHAP-based interpretability analysis further elucidated the distinct features contributing to RP diagnosis and prognosis. While our study demonstrates the value of fundus images in predicting visual outcomes, external validation using diverse datasets is essential for translating these findings into clinical practice.

In the realm of electrocardiogram (ECG) signal processing, accurate and efficient classification is crucial for real-time medical applications and wearable devices. However, existing methods face significant challenges due to noise contamination, limited labeled data, and high model complexity. To address these issues, we propose a novel Quantum Angle-Distance Kernel (QADK) framework that leverages quantum-inspired principles to enhance class resolution while maintaining transparency. QADK represents heartbeats as descriptive features mapped into fixed-length complex vectors using a simulated quantum coding scheme. The encoded patterns are then compared using a balanced mixture of similarity scores and projection gaps, generating a stable kernel matrix compatible with conventional machine learning tools. We evaluate the performance of QADK on a large ECG dataset through 100 Monte-Carlo measurements, comparing it to established methods such as linear SVM, RBF SVM, 1D CNN, MLP, and single-class anomaly detection. Our results demonstrate that QADK-SVM achieves state-of-the-art classification accuracy, with an average AUC-ROC of 0.977 ± 0.003, macro F1 score of 0.923 ± 0.005, and accuracy of 0.937 ± 0.004. Furthermore, we show that both components of the QADK pipeline contribute to its performance, with the fidelity term primarily improving specificity. Theoretical analysis reveals that QADK is compatible with near-term quantum hardware, requiring only 11 qubits and standard swap tests. This makes it an attractive solution for ECG classification in resource-constrained environments, where strong accuracy, high interpretability, and efficient computation are essential.

Here is a rewritten abstract for the machine learning study:

Osteoporosis poses a significant public health concern in aging populations, with diagnosis often relying on dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA), a costly and radiation-based method. This study aimed to develop an interpretable machine learning model that can predict osteoporosis risk using readily available demographic, biochemical, and lifestyle data. The proposed model was designed as an accessible online calculator for rapid clinical and community screening.

To develop the model, we leveraged the Korean National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (KNHANES) dataset from 2008-2011, excluding individuals with over 30% missing data and imputing incomplete variables using polynomial interpolation and mode imputation. Spearman correlation analysis identified osteoporosis-related features, which were then reduced to 15 essential predictors through GradientBoost-RFE and LASSO regression. We trained GradientBoost, CatBoost, and XGBoost algorithms on these predictors to estimate abnormal DXA results and classify bone status.

Our model was evaluated using the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), specificity (SPE), and accuracy (ACC) metrics, with a temporal validation set from the 2008 wave of KNHANES for testing. The optimal model achieved an AUC of 0.845 and SPE of 0.897 for identifying abnormal DXA outcomes, and demonstrated high performance in multiclass classification tasks.

We conclude that our interpretable machine learning model accurately predicts osteoporosis risk and DXA abnormalities using readily available data. To facilitate clinical translation, the model has been deployed as an interactive online calculator, enabling non-invasive, rapid osteoporosis risk assessment without radiological testing. This tool may support early identification of high-risk individuals, optimize DXA utilization, and enhance preventive care strategies across diverse healthcare settings.

The deployment of unsupervised anomaly detection systems in diverse smart home environments is often hampered by the requirement for time-consuming and costly hyperparameter tuning at each site. To address this critical challenge, we investigate the concept of hyperparameter transferability as a means of creating plug-and-play security solutions that require minimal setup. This study systematically evaluates the performance of five prominent unsupervised machine learning models – Elliptic Envelope, Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, One-Class SVM, and Autoencoder – across five widely used IoT datasets.

Our evaluation methodology involves rigorous dataset-specific hyperparameter tuning to establish a baseline for each model's performance. We then compare the results of transferred configurations against both per-dataset optimization and default settings. Our findings indicate that while optimal performance is still achieved through dataset-specific tuning, an intelligent transfer strategy significantly outperforms default configurations in many cases.

Notably, our analysis reveals that the IoTID20 dataset serves as a particularly effective source for hyperparameter transfer due to its high feature space complexity and cluster overlap, which create a challenging yet robust training environment. Furthermore, we identify a strategic trade-off between model performance and ease of use: while Autoencoders and Local Outlier Factor deliver outstanding absolute performance, Isolation Forest offers the most substantial improvement over default settings.

This research provides a quantitative framework for dataset-driven initialization, offering insights that can inform the development of robust, low-maintenance intrusion detection systems. By identifying effective hyperparameter transfer strategies, our study aims to facilitate the widespread adoption of anomaly detection systems in smart home environments.

The early and accurate detection of Alzheimer's disease (AD) remains a critical clinical challenge. Recent advances in machine learning have enabled the development of novel diagnostic models that leverage blood transcriptomic data to identify individuals at risk of developing AD. This study presents an innovative approach, the Lactylation-Derived Score (LDS), which integrates lactylation-related genes from nine AD cohorts using a standardized pipeline incorporating z-score normalization, random forest-based feature selection, plsRglm modeling, and 10-fold cross-validation. The LDS model was externally validated on seven independent brain transcriptomic datasets and clinically evaluated in an external plasma cohort comprising 540 samples. Logistic regression was employed to combine LDS with plasma phosphorylated tau 181 (p-tau181) and p-tau217 biomarkers, yielding a three-marker model that achieved superior diagnostic performance compared to individual components. The results demonstrate the potential of LDS as a scalable and interpretable transcriptomic model for AD diagnosis, complementing existing plasma-based biomarkers and supporting precision medicine approaches in this field. Notably, LDS-high individuals exhibited distinct neuroinflammatory activation and metabolic stress signatures, underscoring its utility in identifying at-risk populations and informing targeted therapeutic interventions.

Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), particularly machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL), have revolutionized various fields, including dentistry. The increasing complexity of patient data and the need for more efficient clinical decision-making processes have prompted the development of AI-driven tools to support dental professionals. This study aimed to design and evaluate an AI-powered tool, HistorAI, that analyzes patient anamnesis forms to generate comprehensive reports and provide treatment suggestions.

To assess the effectiveness of HistorAI, a 22-item anamnesis questionnaire was used to guide structured prompting of two AI models: GPT-4 and Gemini. The integrated API enabled data analysis, treatment suggestion generation, prescription writing, and referral recommendations. Descriptive statistics and independent-samples t-tests were employed to compare evaluation times and outcomes between AI-assisted and conventional methods. Effect sizes were calculated using Cohen's d, with a significance threshold of p < 0.01.

Results indicate that HistorAI successfully evaluates medical and dental histories, identifies contraindicated medications and anesthetics, assesses patient complaints, and provides preliminary treatment recommendations. Notably, the AI-assisted process significantly reduced evaluation time compared to conventional methods (p < 0.01), with a substantial efficiency gain (Cohen's d = 2.599). These findings suggest that HistorAI can enhance clinical decision-making in dental practice while maintaining clinician oversight.

The integration of AI-powered tools like HistorAI into dental workflows holds promise for improving efficiency and patient care. However, further clinical validation and consideration of ethical implications are necessary to ensure the safe and responsible adoption of these technologies in dental settings.

Accurate prediction of novel compound-protein interactions (CPIs) is a critical component of the drug discovery process, enabling researchers to identify potential therapeutic targets with unprecedented speed and efficiency. However, the generalizability of machine learning-based CPI prediction models is severely limited by the availability and diversity of high-quality training datasets. Federated learning (FL), an emerging paradigm for integrating multi-site data while preserving privacy, has been proposed as a solution to this challenge. Nonetheless, its effectiveness in handling heterogeneous data from diverse molecular domains remains poorly understood, hindering its broader adoption in industry settings.

This study investigates the application of FL to CPI prediction using datasets spanning multiple chemical and protein domains, with the aim of providing practical guidance for optimizing the FL approach. Our results indicate that while the FL model exhibits improved out-of-domain prediction performance, it is outperformed by local models on in-domain data under conditions of high data heterogeneity. Building on these findings, we develop a novel strategy to achieve robust performance across both in- and out-of-domain tasks: a similarity-guided ensemble (SGE) that combines the global FL model with fine-tuned models based on each client's local data.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach using real-world industry data, including samples from public databases and proprietary datasets from 13 pharmaceutical companies. Our findings provide practical insights for implementing FL in contemporary drug discovery processes, addressing a critical knowledge gap in the field.

This study investigates the application of machine learning (ML) techniques for predicting gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) risk among pregnant women. A retrospective analysis was conducted on a dataset comprising 3,649 pregnant women from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) database spanning 2007-2018. The dataset included baseline demographic information and dietary nutrient intake data for 45 nutrients. To address class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) was employed after splitting the dataset into training and testing sets. Feature selection was performed using Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) to reduce multicollinearity and the Boruta algorithm to identify core predictors. Six ML models were trained, including XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN). Model performance was evaluated using metrics such as area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-Beta score, and precision-recall AUC. SHAP analysis was conducted to elucidate feature importance. The results indicate that XGBoost outperformed other models in predicting GDM risk, achieving an accuracy of 93.1% and AUC of 0.966 in the validation set. Core predictors identified by the study include demographics, dietary nutrients such as protein, fiber, and carotenoids, as well as lifestyle factors like smoking status and educational level. The findings suggest that ML models integrating demographic and nutritional data can accurately predict GDM risk, supporting early detection and intervention in clinical practice.

The identification and classification of cell types from single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) datasets pose significant challenges in understanding complex biological systems. Recent advances in scRNA-seq have revealed novel cell types and functions, but the lack of robust annotation methods hinders further discovery. To address this limitation, we developed CIA, a computational tool designed to accurately identify cell types across diverse datasets without reliance on fully annotated reference datasets or intricate machine learning processes.

CIA leverages predefined cell type signatures to provide a user-friendly solution for cell-type and functional annotation of single cells. The framework is implemented in both Python and R programming languages, ensuring compatibility with major single-cell analysis frameworks. This accessibility enables researchers to integrate CIA into their existing workflows with minimal additional effort.

Our evaluation demonstrates that CIA achieves classification performances comparable to state-of-the-art approaches while requiring significantly lower computational resources. Furthermore, CIA facilitates reproducible signature-based cell assignments through graphical summaries, empowering researchers to explore the complex transcriptional landscape of single cells with greater ease and precision. By streamlining cell-type identification and annotation, CIA offers a powerful tool for advancing our understanding of biological systems at the cellular level.

This study presents MatterVial, an advanced hybrid framework for feature-based machine learning in materials science. By integrating latent representations from a variety of pretrained graph-neural network (GNN) models with computationally efficient descriptors and novel features derived from symbolic regression, MatterVial significantly expands the feature space available to traditional feature-based models. This approach leverages the chemical transparency of traditional methods while harnessing the predictive power of deep learning architectures. When applied to Matbench tasks, MatterVial demonstrates substantial error reductions and enhances the performance of MODNet, a state-of-the-art feature-based model, to rival that of end-to-end GNNs in several instances, with accuracy increases exceeding 40% for multiple tasks. Furthermore, an integrated interpretability module enables the decoding of latent GNN-derived descriptors into explicit, physically meaningful formulas through the use of surrogate models and symbolic regression. By providing a high-performance tool that aligns with explainable AI principles, MatterVial advances materials informatics and facilitates more targeted and autonomous materials discovery.

The diagnosis and prognosis of Alzheimer's Disease (AD) have been increasingly reliant on artificial intelligence (AI) models that integrate multimodal data from various sources. However, the lack of standardization and transparency in these approaches hinders their interpretability and clinical adoption. This study aims to address this gap by systematically reviewing recent advances in AI-driven multimodal fusion for AD prediction. A comprehensive analysis of widely used datasets is presented, including an examination of their modalities, preprocessing pipelines, and accessibility to ensure reproducibility and methodological transparency. The review categorizes data harmonization and preprocessing techniques across neuroimaging (fMRI, sMRI, PET), electrophysiological (EEG), and genomic modalities, highlighting domain-specific practices and challenges. Fusion strategies are classified into four paradigms: data-level, feature-level, decision-level, and temporal (early, intermediate, late) approaches, which are evaluated for their implementation and diagnostic impact. Furthermore, the adoption of explainable AI (XAI) techniques is investigated across studies, revealing a significant underrepresentation of works that prioritize multimodality, explainability, and methodological rigor. This study adheres to PRISMA and Kitchenham's guidelines to ensure transparency and replicability in evidence synthesis. The review concludes with recommendations for future research aimed at developing robust, interpretable, and clinically relevant AI models for AD diagnosis and prognosis.

The inverse design of novel materials is increasingly reliant on machine learning-driven approaches to navigate the vast composition space. However, these methods are often hindered by two primary limitations: (i) their tendency to suffer from prediction biases in high-dimensional spaces, and (ii) their inability to effectively incorporate domain expert knowledge into the design process. To address these challenges, we propose AIMATDESIGN, a reinforcement learning framework that leverages difference-based sampling to augment experimental data and construct a robust experience pool. A novel automated refinement strategy, guided by large language models, is employed to correct prediction inconsistencies and ensure alignment between reward signals and value functions. Furthermore, a knowledge-based reward mechanism is integrated to leverage expert rules and enhance stability and efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that AIMATDESIGN outperforms conventional machine learning and reinforcement learning baselines in terms of discovery efficiency, convergence speed, and success rates. Notably, the framework's ability to capture property trends is exemplified by the experimental validation of a Zr-based alloy exhibiting exceptional mechanical properties, including 1.7 GPa yield strength and 10.2% elongation. The proposed framework provides a reliable pathway toward closed-loop, data-efficient materials discovery, underscoring its potential for transformative impact in the field.

In the field of electroencephalography (EEG)-based analysis, particularly in neurolinguistics and second language acquisition, feature selection plays a pivotal role in optimizing machine learning models. Understanding neural activity during language learning offers valuable insights into cognitive and linguistic processing. Despite significant advancements in EEG-based studies, identifying the most effective feature selection techniques to enhance classification performance and interpretability remains an open challenge. This study systematically evaluates and compares multiple feature selection methods to improve the accuracy and efficiency of EEG-based classification during the acquisition of Arabic and Hindi vocabulary.

EEG signals were recorded from twenty participants as they learned new words, and the data underwent preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification using a range of machine learning algorithms, including Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree (DT), XGBoost, CatBoost, and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN). Five feature selection techniques – Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (mRMR), refined selection, ReliefF, ensemble selection, and Genetic Algorithm (GA) – were applied and compared. The results demonstrate that feature selection significantly enhances classification performance, with ensemble and ReliefF achieving the highest accuracies of 97.13% and 97.15%, respectively, particularly when combined with RF, SVM, and XGBoost classifiers.

These findings indicate that effective dimensionality reduction, mitigation of overfitting, and enhanced model interpretability can be achieved through judicious application of feature selection techniques. This systematic evaluation provides valuable insights into neural mechanisms of language learning and supports advancements in brain–computer interfaces, cognitive neuroscience, and machine learning applications.

In the realm of computational chemistry, a comprehensive understanding of proton transfer mechanisms is crucial for elucidating polar reaction mechanisms. To address this knowledge gap, we have compiled an extensive dataset comprising over 51 million kinetically plausible proton transfer steps between heteroatoms from approximately 8,000 acids and conjugate bases with experimentally determined aqueous pKa values spanning a wide range (−15 to +37). By leveraging a simplified Eigen equation, we estimated rate factors at 25 °C using only pKa values without incorporating statistical factors. Our dataset was further augmented by including an additional 5,043 proton transfer steps from carbon acids to heteroatom bases, calculated using the Eigen-Bernasconi equation and reported intrinsic rate constants along with Brønsted β values. To ensure machine learning compatibility, each entry was encoded in SMIRKS format with electron-flow specification. Prioritizing structural diversity over environmental conditions, our dataset is expected to provide accurate predictions of aqueous proton transfer rates. The compiled dataset offers a valuable resource for training models to predict stepwise mechanistic pathways, thereby enhancing the understanding of polar reaction mechanisms and facilitating the development of more accurate computational tools in chemistry.

In the realm of biological and biomedical research, elucidating the relationships between metabolites and biochemical pathways is crucial for interpreting molecular datasets. However, pathway annotations are often scarce in these datasets, hindering their utility for comprehensive analysis. To address this limitation, previous studies have leveraged machine learning models to predict pathway associations using data from the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG). Nevertheless, other knowledgebases like MetaCyc offer a vast repository of compound entries and pathway definitions, warranting exploration.

This study extends the existing research by developing and evaluating multilayer perceptron models on compound entries and pathway annotations derived from MetaCyc. The results demonstrate that these models achieve a mean Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) of 0.845 with a standard deviation of 0.0101, comparable to the performance observed with KEGG data. Notably, when focusing on metabolic pathways, the MCC for MetaCyc's predictions reaches 0.800 with a standard deviation of 0.021, representing an improvement of over 5.6% compared to KEGG's metabolic-only pathway predictions.

These findings indicate that the MetaCyc dataset can be effectively utilized at the current state-of-the-art performance level, suggesting that its pathways can be predicted with similar accuracy as those from KEGG. The results underscore the potential of leveraging alternative knowledgebases in machine learning-based pathway annotation and prediction tasks.

This study investigates the representation learning of hypergraphs, a type of complex network that can capture intricate relationships among nodes. Unlike traditional networks, hypergraphs enable the modeling of multiple interactions between nodes, rendering them suitable for applications such as social network analysis and recommendation systems. However, existing node embedding methods often neglect the structural properties inherent to hypergraphs, leading to suboptimal performance in downstream tasks.

To address this limitation, we propose HyperS2V, a novel approach that focuses on preserving structural similarity within hypergraphs. Our method involves two key components: first, the definition of hyper-degrees to quantify nodes' structural characteristics; second, the development of multi-scale random walk framework for generating node embeddings. These embeddings are then evaluated using both synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating superior performance in terms of interpretability and applicability to downstream tasks compared to existing methods. The results highlight the potential of HyperS2V as a robust tool for hypergraph representation learning and its utility in various machine learning applications.

In the field of computer vision, single image super-resolution (SISR) is a pivotal task that enables the recovery of high-quality images from their low-resolution counterparts. This technique has far-reaching applications in various domains, including facial recognition, video surveillance, medical imaging, remote sensing, and scene text recognition. While conventional SISR methods often struggle to accurately restore fine details, recent advancements in machine learning-based algorithms have shown promising results, particularly those leveraging residual networks. However, these approaches can be computationally intensive due to their large architecture size. To address this issue, we introduce a novel framework, Multi-Scale Deep Residual Networks (MSDRNs), which incorporates mean-std normalization to ensure uniform statistical scaling of residual branches. Additionally, we employ Bayesian hyperparameter optimization to adaptively tune critical parameters and achieve robust convergence. Our approach combines the evaluation of full-reference metrics (PSNR and SSIM) with no-reference NIQE index to comprehensively assess image quality. The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in terms of both quantitative accuracy and human-visual fidelity, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches such as SwinIR and EDSR. By exploiting high-resolution images for accuracy maximization, MSDRNs offer a more efficient and effective solution for SISR tasks.

This study contributes to the development of artificial intelligence (AI) solutions for male fertility diagnostics by introducing a comprehensive collection of expert-labeled image datasets. The datasets comprise three distinct categories, each designed to address specific challenges in automated sperm analysis. The raw dataset consists of over 400 high-resolution images of stained bright-field samples containing multiple sperm cells, while the binary classification set comprises cropped image patches labeled as either sperm or non-sperm. Furthermore, a multiclass dataset categorizes individual sperm cells based on DNA fragmentation level, with annotations ranging from large to no halo size.

The datasets were annotated by five experienced embryologists, with final labels determined through majority agreement. This rigorous annotation process ensures the accuracy and reliability of the data, providing a robust foundation for training and benchmarking machine learning models. The availability of these datasets is open, facilitating reproducibility and enabling further research in reproductive health. By leveraging this collection, researchers can develop and evaluate AI-powered systems for sperm detection, classification, and chromatin integrity assessment, ultimately contributing to improved male fertility diagnostics.

This dataset addresses a critical gap in multimodal biomechanical recordings during various locomotor tasks by providing synchronized measurements of electromyographic (EMG), inertial (IMU), and plantar pressure data. A total of 45 adults, comprising 15 individuals with unilateral transtibial amputation and 30 without amputation, participated in the study, completing five standardized tasks: level walking, ramp ascent/descent, and stair ascent/descent. Each participant underwent 50 supervised trials, facilitating a comprehensive understanding of gait adaptation across terrains and populations.

The dataset was collected using wireless EMG and IMU sensors (Delsys Trigno Avanti) to measure muscle activation and kinematics, as well as intelligent insoles (XSENSOR) to capture plantar pressure distribution. The raw data were stored in.hpf and.XSN formats, with processed outputs available in.csv files, organized by task and sensor type along with complete participant metadata.

Key dataset outputs include time-normalized EMG amplitudes, segment kinematics, and pressure maps across various terrains and populations. Technical and experimental validation of the dataset was conducted during data acquisition to ensure its reliability and accuracy. This comprehensive resource enables quantitative analysis of gait adaptation and supports machine learning approaches for locomotion classification, thereby facilitating research in biomechanics, rehabilitation engineering, robotics, and clinical gait studies. The dataset is provided in accessible formats to promote reuse and foster innovative applications in these fields.

This study investigates the feasibility of implementing continual learning in quantum machine learning systems, which are poised to surpass classical computers on certain tasks. However, these systems often suffer from catastrophic forgetting, a phenomenon where previously learned information is lost when new data is introduced. To address this challenge, we employed an experimental setup involving a superconducting processor and sequentially trained a quantum classifier on three distinct tasks: image classification and quantum state categorization. Our results show that the quantum classifier exhibited significant catastrophic forgetting, with accuracy dropping to 70% after the third task. Building upon the elastic weight consolidation strategy, we successfully adapted the quantum classifier to incrementally retain knowledge across tasks, achieving an average accuracy of 95.1%. Furthermore, our experiments demonstrated that a quantum classifier can outperform its classical counterpart on sequential tasks involving quantum-engineered data, with comparable model complexity. These findings provide a crucial step towards developing adaptable quantum learning systems capable of handling complex, dynamic environments.

Categorical feature encoding is a crucial step in machine learning pipelines, as it enables numerical inputs for models that often rely on them. However, traditional methods like one-hot encoding can be suboptimal due to increased dimensionality and lack of consideration for inherent relationships between categories. This study investigates 26 categorical encoding techniques across 13 real-world datasets and 7 machine learning algorithms, categorizing the methods based on predictive task type, model performance, and computational efficiency. Our taxonomy provides a framework for selecting encoders that balance these competing factors. Furthermore, we demonstrate how Safe AI metrics can be integrated into encoding pipelines to assess model robustness and fairness, offering complementary insights beyond traditional evaluation metrics. To facilitate further research and practical application, we introduce EncodeHero: a Python tool allowing users to (1) extend our benchmark with their own datasets and (2) select the most suitable encoding method based on their specific needs and constraints. Our findings contribute to a more informed approach to categorical feature encoding in machine learning, enabling researchers and practitioners to develop more accurate and robust models.

Characterization of Serum Metabolomic Profiles in Acute Pancreatitis and Development of Bile Acid-Based Predictive Models Using Artificial Intelligence.

Acute pancreatitis (AP) is a complex and potentially life-threatening condition, necessitating the identification of reliable diagnostic biomarkers for early prediction of severe AP (SAP). The current study aimed to investigate serum metabolomic profiles in patients with distinct disease phases and integrate metabolomics with artificial intelligence to construct predictive models. A multicentre observational protocol was conducted, involving comparative metabolomic analysis of 303 metabolites and 461 lipid species in AP patients. Weighted gene coexpression network analysis revealed significant correlations between clinical parameters and specific metabolic clusters, particularly bile acids (BAs) and lipid species. Targeted quantification of 63 BAs was performed within a validation cohort (n = 948), facilitating the derivation of two distinct BA panels using machine learning algorithms. The first panel, comprising nine BAs, demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy for AP, including among individuals with negative conventional enzymatic biomarkers, and effectively discriminated AP from acute cholangitis. A second panel, consisting of 13 BAs, reliably identified patients at elevated risk for SAP progression. These findings validate the translational potential of machine learning-driven metabolic biomarkers in acute abdominal conditions and underscore the clinical utility of BAs as promising diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers in AP.

The clinical heterogeneity of prostate cancer (PCa) remains a significant challenge in the field. To address this issue, we employed a multi-omics approach combining single-cell and spatial transcriptomics with explainable machine learning to identify key drivers of lethal tumor behavior. Our analysis of 141,986 high-quality single cells from localized, hormone-sensitive, and castration-resistant PCa revealed a distinct malignant epithelial subpopulation characterized by elevated chromosomal instability, androgen receptor activation, cell-cycle dysregulation, and stemness potential. Spatial mapping further elucidated the presence of immune-enriched yet suppressive niches, where fibroblasts and myeloid cells coexist with exhausted lymphocytes, indicating a functional imbalance in the tumor microenvironment.

Using 101 machine learning pipelines, we identified a Lasso plus PLS-Cox model that demonstrated strong concordance across independent cohorts. The resulting C4-based risk score independently predicted recurrence-free survival after adjustment for age, Gleason score, and T stage. A nomogram combining this score with clinical variables showed good discrimination. SHAP interpretation highlighted MT1M, PCSK1N, and ACSL3 as major risk-driving features. Notably, PCSK1N was found to be progressively upregulated from normal prostate tissue to castration-resistant disease, promoting proliferation, clonogenicity, migration, and enzalutamide resistance. Inhibition of PCSK1N sensitized organoids and xenografts to AR-targeted therapy.

Our findings provide a C4-centered framework for understanding lethal tumor behavior in PCa and offer an experimentally supported approach for prognostic stratification. This study highlights the potential of multi-omics analysis combined with explainable machine learning to identify key drivers of cancer progression and inform targeted therapeutic strategies.

The accurate prediction of global natural gas trade is essential for countries to optimize their energy strategies and ensure energy security. Traditional forecasting methods, relying on expert knowledge and statistical techniques, have limitations in capturing the complexity and uncertainty inherent in global natural gas trade. The incorporation of machine learning (ML) approaches has shown promise in modeling these complex factors using historical data. However, existing ML models struggle to account for three unique characteristics of global natural gas trade: network relationships between countries, dynamic changes over time, and influences from numerous complex factors.

To address this gap, a novel hybrid ML model is proposed, combining Graph Attention Networks (GAT), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and Transformer architectures. This integrated framework, termed GAT-GT, leverages the strengths of each component: GAT learns annual trade network features, GRU models dynamic relationships, and Transformer combines complex factors with latent features for forecasting. The proposed model benefits from the comprehensive capture of global natural gas trade characteristics, including network, dynamic, and complex-factor influences.

Empirical studies utilizing global natural gas trade data from 2000 to 2023 demonstrate that GAT-GT significantly outperforms eight advanced ML competitors in forecasting accuracy. This research contributes to the development of more effective ML models for predicting global natural gas trade, enabling countries to make informed decisions about their energy strategies and ensuring a more secure energy future.

The accurate modeling of crystalline materials is crucial for elucidating structure-property relationships in critical materials, thereby accelerating materials discovery. However, recent graph neural networks (GNNs) often struggle to provide physical interpretability and fail to explicitly model the hierarchical and symmetrical nature of crystals. To address this limitation, we propose a novel deep learning framework called Capsule Graph Networks with E(3)-Equivariance (CGN-e3). This framework integrates equivariant message passing with capsule networks to capture both geometric symmetries and hierarchical motif structures. CGN-e3 leverages E(3)-equivariant message passing to learn physically consistent features, which are then organized into capsule representations that can disentangle local motifs and connect them to global properties. We evaluate the effectiveness of our framework on bandgap and formation energy prediction, as well as material classification using the Materials Project and Matbench datasets. Our results demonstrate that CGN-e3 achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.054 eV/atom and 0.379 eV for formation energy and bandgap prediction, respectively, outperforming CGCNN and matching the performance of MEGNet on the same dataset. Furthermore, our framework provides insightful interpretations of the learned capsule representations, offering an unsupervised pathway for interpretable motif discovery. The dynamic routing-by-agreement mechanism identifies and aggregates structurally significant local environments into higher-order graph-level capsules, yielding human-intelligible insights by explicitly quantifying the contribution of specific structural motifs to target material properties.

This study investigates the classification of healthy controls and patients with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) or schizophrenia based on their oculomotor behavior during active visual fixation tasks. Abnormalities in inhibitory control mechanisms, which are essential for cognitive function, have been linked to these neurological disorders. Eye movement studies and machine learning techniques have been employed to differentiate clinical from neurotypical populations. In this research, data from three visual fixation tasks were analyzed using statistical tests to identify saccade features relevant to classification. A shallow Artificial Neural Network (ANN) was implemented for binary and three-class classification. The results demonstrate that selected saccadic features can distinguish between healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia or OCD with moderate accuracy. Notably, the findings suggest that group differences are primarily driven by intrinsic properties of saccadic generation rather than fixation or inhibitory mechanisms. These insights provide preliminary evidence on the potential use of machine learning-based classification methods for distinguishing between neurological disorders based on oculomotor behavior. The study's results contribute to a better understanding of the underlying neurophysiological mechanisms and highlight the need for further research in this area.

The accurate detection of depression through speech or conversation has become increasingly important in recent years due to the growing need for effective mental health assessment tools. However, current methods often fall short in terms of effectiveness and interpretability, particularly when dealing with diverse languages and datasets. This study addresses these limitations by proposing a novel model that combines multiple feature extraction and selection techniques to enhance performance.

The proposed method employs an adaptive threshold-based pre-processing technique to eliminate irrelevant information, followed by the application of a twinned Savitzky-Golay filter to minimize noise in the dataset. A Synchro-Squeezed Adaptive Wavelet Transform Algorithm is then used to transform the signal into an image, which is subsequently analyzed using the Singular Empirical Decomposition and Sparse Autoencoder model.

The extracted features are then fused using a Weighted Soft Attention-based Fusion model, before being refined by the Chaotic Mud Ring Optimization algorithm. Finally, a Dilated Convolutional Neural Network based Bidirectional-Long Short Term Memory-Bi-LSTM architecture is utilized to detect different stages of depression, achieving state-of-the-art results.

The proposed method was evaluated on three benchmark datasets: DAIC-WOZ, AVEC 2019, and MELD. The results demonstrate a significant improvement in detection accuracy, with an F1-score of 93.22%, precision of 93.11%, recall of 93.12%, and accuracy of 93.31% on the DAIC-WOZ dataset. Furthermore, the model achieved high accuracy rates of 93.91% and 85.34% on the AVEC 2019 and MELD datasets, respectively. These findings indicate that the proposed method has potential for real-world applications in mental health assessment.

Recent advancements in Camouflaged Object Detection (COD) have been driven by its diverse practical applications, necessitating the development of more accurate and efficient detection methods. This study presents a novel human-machine collaboration framework for COD, capitalizing on the synergistic benefits of computer vision models and noninvasive brain-computer interfaces. The proposed approach incorporates a multiview backbone to quantify uncertainty in model predictions, utilizing this uncertainty during training to enhance efficiency and accuracy. Furthermore, low-confidence cases are deferred to human evaluation via RSVP-based BCIs during testing, facilitating more reliable decision-making processes. Experimental evaluations on the CAMO dataset demonstrate the efficacy of our framework, achieving state-of-the-art results with significant improvements in balanced accuracy (4.56%) and F1 score (3.66%). Notably, for top-performing participants, enhancements reached 7.6% in balanced accuracy and 6.66% in F1 score. Analysis of training data reveals a strong correlation between confidence and precision, while ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of our training policy and human-machine collaboration strategy. This research contributes to reducing human cognitive load, enhancing system reliability, and establishing a foundation for advancements in real-world COD applications and human-computer interaction.

Machine learning models have been increasingly employed in predicting acute myeloid leukemia (AML), a clinically aggressive hematologic malignancy driven by complex genetic and epigenetic alterations. Recent studies have highlighted the potential of circular RNAs (circRNAs) as diagnostic biomarkers, but existing predictive models primarily rely on differential expression without considering the impact of higher-order chromatin organization on circRNA formation and function. This study proposes a novel machine learning framework that incorporates three-dimensional (3D) genome architecture to refine circRNA selection for AML prediction. By mapping 9,565 circRNAs onto a reconstructed 3D chromatin model derived from high-throughput chromosome conformation capture data, we identified spatial clustering patterns and biological pathway enrichment among the circRNAs. Our analysis revealed that eighteen pathways exhibited significant 3D aggregation of circRNAs, enabling radial stratification based on nuclear localization. To develop predictive models, five circRNA panels were designed using complementary strategies combining expression, pathway, and spatial features. Cross-validation and external validation across six machine learning algorithms demonstrated that the panel derived from the fifth radial layer (Panel-3DG-Radius5) achieved the most robust and consistent performance, with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve exceeding 0.99. The integration of 3D genomic context reduced feature collinearity while enhancing biological interpretability, establishing a paradigm for circRNA biomarker discovery that leverages spatial genome organization to improve the precision and robustness of AML predictive modeling.

Here's a rewritten version of the abstract:

The development of brain-controlled wheelchair (BCW) technology has revolutionized mobility assistance for individuals with motor impairments, enabling direct control via non-invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs). Motor imagery-based BCIs (MI-BCIs), which rely on neural activity associated with imagined movements, have gained popularity due to their intuitive and stimulus-free nature. However, achieving reliable multi-class classification accuracy in MI-BCI systems remains a significant challenge. To address this issue, we propose an advanced feature extraction algorithm, EHT-CSP, which integrates Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition Hilbert-Huang Transform (EEMD-HHT) with Time-Frequency Common Spatial Pattern (TFCSP). Our approach extracts marginal spectrum entropy and energy spectrum entropy using EEMD-HHT, followed by the combination of these features with TFCSP-derived feature vectors to enhance classification performance. We employ Light Gradient Boosting Machine for classification and evaluate our proposed MI-BCI system through both offline analysis and real-world BCW obstacle avoidance experiments. Results demonstrate that EHT-CSP achieves an average classification accuracy of 78.45%, with all participants successfully completing BCW navigation tasks, outperforming existing models in both efficacy and robustness.

In the context of the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT), where medical devices and real-time monitoring nodes generate highly skewed and burst-like traffic, traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS) often struggle to accurately model these patterns. Specifically, Gaussian Naïve Bayes classifiers have been shown to underperform due to their reliance on symmetric feature distributions, which do not hold in IoMT networks.
