This study addresses this limitation by introducing NBGamma, a modified Naïve Bayes classifier that incorporates a Gamma-based likelihood formulation to better capture positively skewed traffic features. The proposed approach mathematically integrates the Gamma distribution into the likelihood computation and formulates the IDS task as a security-driven optimization problem. Experimental evaluation on the CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset demonstrates significant improvements in precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC compared to Gaussian Naïve Bayes under both unbalanced and SMOTE-balanced settings. The results highlight the potential of NBGamma to enhance lightweight IDS modeling for resource-constrained IoMT environments, enabling more effective detection of malicious traffic patterns and advancing security-driven optimization in healthcare networks. Overall, this study contributes to the development of robust and reliable intrusion detection systems for IoMT applications.

Alzheimer's disease is a debilitating neurodegenerative disorder that affects cognitive function by impairing neurotransmitters, neurons, and tissue in the human brain. Despite the lack of an effective cure, medications can slow disease progression. Early detection of Alzheimer's disease is critical for treatment and further research, but current challenges include limited trained samples and excessive feature descriptions when applying different classification algorithms. Moreover, there is a scarcity of reliable AI-based solutions for detecting this disease. To address these issues, we developed a system utilizing supervised machine learning models to facilitate the initial detection of Alzheimer's disease through a web application. Specifically, we employed five classification algorithms: Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, and K-Nearest Neighbors, on a dataset obtained from Kaggle featuring 35 attributes and 2149 data points. To optimize performance, we balanced the dataset and evaluated feature importance. Our results indicate that the Random Forest model achieved the highest accuracy of 91.19%, providing a promising approach for early Alzheimer's disease detection.

This study assesses the effectiveness of a novel, compact, and lightweight dual-band switched-beam antenna designed for 5G sub-6 GHz communications, operating at 0.7 GHz and 2.6 GHz. The proposed antenna structure incorporates innovative features such as grooves and parasitic elements to enhance bandwidth performance. To optimize seven critical dimensional parameters, three machine learning models – decision tree, random forest, and XGBoost – are employed to facilitate broadband operation. A comprehensive dataset of 244 samples is generated from 305 simulated scenarios created using CST Microwave Studio, simulating a wide range of conditions targeting resonance frequencies and corresponding bandwidths. The incorporation of a short-circuit mechanism enables beam steering in two directions across both frequency bands. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed design achieves significant improvements in bandwidth, with enhancements of 11.52 MHz (113.72%) at 0.63 GHz and 128.72 MHz (200.59%) at 2.48 GHz compared to a conventional reference antenna. Empirical measurements validate the simulation outcomes, confirming the antenna's ability to switch beams effectively and operate in wideband dual-band mode. The proposed design's simplicity, compactness, and machine learning-driven performance make it an attractive alternative for next-generation wireless systems.

Predictive modeling of Patient Length of Stay (LOS) in resource-limited settings is a complex task, influenced by data skewness, outlier sensitivity, and imbalanced datasets. In such contexts, machine learning (ML) offers potential solutions that require validation through rigorous evaluation. This study aims to develop and assess the effectiveness of ML models for predicting prolonged LOS (≥ 6 days) at Debre Markos Comprehensive Specialized Hospital in Ethiopia, leveraging electronic health records (EHRs) to optimize operational planning.

A retrospective analysis of 27,268 patient records from four hospital wards (2017–2024) was conducted. Data preprocessing involved feature engineering, one-hot encoding, and addressing class imbalance using the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). Eight ML models were trained and evaluated using a range of metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC, with 5-fold cross-validation to ensure robustness. Feature importance was assessed via permutation analysis.

The study found that SMOTE significantly improved model performance, particularly in terms of recall and F1-scores. Gradient Boosting emerged as the top-performing model (AUC: 0.70, Accuracy: 81%, Precision: 72%, Recall: 63%, F1-score: 0.76). Key predictors of prolonged LOS included ward type, age, pregnancy-related conditions, respiratory/digestive diagnoses, and rural residence.

This research demonstrates the potential of ML-driven LOS prediction in resource-constrained settings using EHR data. The findings have implications for optimizing bed management, staffing, and resource allocation, aligning with Ethiopia's Health Sector Transformation Plan (HSTP) goals for efficient healthcare delivery. Future work should focus on real-time validation and integration into hospital systems to maximize the impact of ML-driven LOS prediction.

This study addresses a critical challenge in understanding the strengthening mechanisms of high-entropy alloys (HEAs), which have garnered significant attention due to their potential for enhanced mechanical properties. Conventional theories of solid solution strengthening are insufficient to capture the complex interactions between alloy components and dislocations in HEAs. To bridge this knowledge gap, we develop a data-driven framework that leverages large-scale molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the intrinsic strength of face-centered cubic (FCC) HEAs.

Our approach involves computing dislocation mobility under various temperatures and compositions, revealing intricate glide behavior characterized by jerky and wavy motion due to localized pinning effects. By extracting critical resolved shear stress (CRSS) values at 0 K from these simulations, we identify a significant correlation between CRSS and the standard deviation of atomic pinning strength.

Building on this insight, we propose a set of atomic features that capture local structural and compositional fluctuations in HEAs. These features are then used to construct a symbolic model, which predicts the variability of atomic pinning strength using the Sure Independence Screening and Sparsifying Operator method. Our framework provides a unique combination of mechanistic understanding and predictive capabilities, enabling the design of strong, compositionally complex alloys with enhanced mechanical properties.

Automated grading of essays has emerged as a crucial component in enhancing the efficiency of English education by mitigating human bias and increasing scalability in assessment. However, conventional rule-based and machine learning methods often fall short due to their inherent limitations. This study proposes a novel hybrid scoring model that integrates Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi LSTM), Transformer encoder, and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (Light GBM) to overcome these weaknesses. The Deep Essays Dataset, comprising diverse student populations with human-annotated scores, is utilized as the basis for this research.

The proposed approach involves a multi-faceted text processing strategy that includes deep preprocessing, shallow and semantic feature extraction, and context representation fusion facilitated by Bi LSTM and Transformer layers. These features are then fed into a Light GBM regressor to predict final scores. Large-scale experiments demonstrate that the hybrid model significantly outperforms existing methods on Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Pearson correlation metrics. Moreover, evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score consistently exceed 99%, underscoring the stability of the proposed system. Graphical analysis using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Precision-Recall (PR) plots further attest to the robustness of the model.

The findings of this study demonstrate the efficacy of combining deep neural networks and gradient boosting in identifying linguistic and semantic patterns within essays. This innovative approach has the potential to revolutionize automated scoring technology, leading to more equitable, reliable, and scalable assessment in English learning.

The development of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) often goes unnoticed until complications arise, underscoring the need for early detection and prevention through transparent machine learning models. This study aimed to develop and evaluate an interpretable Extra Trees Classifier (ETC) for early T2DM risk prediction within the PERSIAN Dena Cohort, prioritizing probability calibration, fairness, and clinical interpretability.

A dataset comprising 3,203 adults aged 35-70 years was analyzed, with seventy-nine variables initially considered before feature elimination retained fifteen informative predictors. The ETC model was optimized through randomized hyperparameter search and evaluated using ten-fold cross-validation with an internal-external split (80/20). Isotonic regression was employed to calibrate probability estimates, while SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Morris sensitivity analysis were used to examine model transparency and feature influence.

The study found that the ETC exhibited moderate discrimination and stable internal consistency, with cross-validated performance metrics indicating mean accuracy of 0.69 ± 0.03 and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.69 ± 0.04. However, calibration revealed under-confident probability estimates following isotonic regression. Sensitivity analyses identified fasting blood sugar, fatty-liver status, age, kidney-stone history, and triglycerides as dominant predictors, with lifestyle factors exerting secondary influence.

The calibrated ETC provided transparent insights into feature interactions and data limitations, underscoring the importance of interpretability and fairness in trustworthy clinical AI. While predictive power was limited, this study highlights the need for further research to expand predictor diversity, address class imbalance, and validate across other PERSIAN cohorts to develop a more generalizable and interpretable model for early T2DM risk prediction.

The proliferation of online information has necessitated the development of sophisticated tools for filtering and generating personalized recommendations, thereby enhancing decision-making in the e-commerce sector. Recommender systems (RSs), which analyze individual user preferences, past reviews, and product ratings, have become indispensable components of modern e-commerce platforms. This study undertakes a comprehensive examination of RSs by reviewing research articles published between 2020 and 2025 from various journals and conferences. The review focuses on emerging techniques aimed at improving system performance, including the application of machine learning, deep learning, large language models, and conversational recommender systems. By exploring these cutting-edge methodologies, this study seeks to address significant research gaps and identify key factors influencing RS platforms. A thorough analysis of existing literature and prevailing research challenges provides a foundation for understanding the current state and future direction of RSs in the e-commerce industry. The findings of this study are expected to provide valuable insights into the evolution of RSs and inform the development of more effective recommendation systems.

The increasing reliance on artificial intelligence in medical diagnosis has led to a growing interest in leveraging machine learning and deep learning algorithms for classifying pulmonary diseases. Spirometry, a widely used lung function test, plays a crucial role in early disease detection and monitoring. This study investigates the performance of various machine learning and deep learning models in distinguishing between different pulmonary conditions using standardized spirometry features from the Global Lung Initiative 2012 dataset.

To achieve this objective, we employed a range of classification algorithms, including logistic regression, support vector machines, k-nearest neighbors, random forests, XGBoost, as well as deep neural networks and convolutional neural networks. Preprocessing and standardization techniques were applied to enhance model accuracy. The performance of each model was evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, F1-macro, and the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve.

Our results indicate that tree-based models exhibit superior generalization performance on a subject-disjoint test set. Specifically, the XGBoost model achieved an accuracy of 99.24 ± 0.59%, followed by the random forest model at 97.23 ± 2.04%. In contrast, deep learning models, such as deep neural networks and convolutional neural networks, yielded lower accuracy rates of 81.61 ± 5.83% and 77.57 ± 5.55%, respectively.

This study highlights the potential of machine learning-based approaches in early disease diagnosis. However, further optimization is required to improve model performance. The findings have significant implications for integrating artificial intelligence-supported spirometry analysis into clinical practice, underscoring the need for validation on larger patient cohorts to ensure seamless integration into healthcare systems.

The integration of machine learning and chemistry through molecular representation learning (MRL) has become a pivotal area of research, with significant implications for predicting molecular properties and facilitating complex tasks such as drug discovery. However, existing MRL methods often falter when confronted with out-of-distribution (OOD) data, which is a common occurrence in real-world molecular environments characterized by dynamic and uncertain conditions. To address this limitation, we present EISG, an innovative framework designed to enhance the generalization capability of MRL models on OOD data. By leveraging environmental inference and subgraph generation techniques, EISG aims to capture the invariant properties of molecular graphs across diverse environments. Specifically, our approach involves the development of an unsupervised environmental classification model that identifies latent variables generated by different distributions, as well as a subgraph extractor based on information bottleneck theory that extracts invariant representations from molecular graphs closely related to prediction labels. Through the combination of novel learning objectives and the collaborative work of the environmental classifier and subgraph extractor, EISG enables MRL models to identify invariant graph representations in various environments, thereby achieving more robust OOD generalization. Experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of our framework across multiple OOD settings, underscoring its potential for improving the reliability and accuracy of molecular representation learning.

The increasing reliance on Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms in various domains has highlighted the critical importance of high-quality training data. However, the availability of large-scale datasets is often hindered by strict privacy regulations, resulting in reduced dataset sizes and under-representation of certain demographic groups. This data scarcity can be detrimental to AI performance, as it perpetuates existing biases and limitations within the algorithms. To mitigate this issue, researchers have explored synthetic data generation techniques that create artificial samples with similar properties to real-world data. This study examines the current state of synthetic tabular data generation methods, focusing on statistical, probabilistic, and Machine Learning-based approaches. A comprehensive analysis of high-quality peer-reviewed articles reveals that synthetic data not only increases dataset size but also enhances its quality by promoting diversity and representation of various demographic groups. Notably, techniques such as Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and its variants have demonstrated significant performance improvements in classification tasks, with reported gains up to 12%. However, the efficacy of synthetic data generation ultimately depends on the underlying process, characteristics of real-world data, and evaluation metrics used to assess quality.

The metabolic reprogramming of cancer cells is characterized by altered lactate accumulation, but its spatial and cell-type-specific effects in lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) remain poorly understood. This study integrates single-cell transcriptomics, spatial transcriptomics, spatial metabolomics, and immunofluorescence with large-scale clinical data to elucidate the role of lactate in shaping the tumor microenvironment. By analyzing high-lactate tumors, we observe increased epithelial and fibroblast populations, whereas low-lactate samples exhibit enriched T/NK cells and monocytes/macrophages. Spatial metabolomics reveals cell-type-specific lactate and pyruvate distributions, with endothelial cells showing minimal lactate accumulation. Notably, endothelial subclusters in high-lactate tissues display angiogenic and stress-response signatures, which are strongly associated with poor prognosis. Machine-learning models consistently identify endothelial and fibroblast programs as key determinants of high-lactate states and adverse clinical outcomes. Our multi-omics spatial profiling demonstrates that lactate plays a pivotal role in driving angiogenesis, immune suppression, and prognostic stratification in LUAD, highlighting lactate-centered pathways as promising therapeutic targets for improving patient outcomes.

The increasing prevalence of herbal medicines and natural dietary supplements has heightened concerns about herb-drug interactions (HDIs) in modern pharmacotherapy. HDIs can significantly impact drug efficacy or safety by altering pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic processes. However, the complexity and cost of experimental validation have hindered comprehensive understanding of these interactions. To address this knowledge gap, we present a novel graph-based computational framework for predicting the effects of HDIs. Our approach constructs a heterogeneous, multi-relational graph incorporating drug-drug interactions (DDIs), drug-target interactions (DTIs), and herb-target interactions (HTIs). By employing metapath-guided random walks to generate node embeddings, we capture the intricate relationships within this complex graph. These embeddings are then integrated with molecular fingerprints, tabular descriptors, and target node encodings to train various supervised classifiers. Our extensive experimental evaluation demonstrates the efficacy of this approach, particularly with tree-based models such as XGBoost and Random Forest, which achieve high predictive performance in multi-class classification tasks. The results underscore the potential of AI-powered graph-based methodologies to support integrative and precision medicine by facilitating scalable and accurate prediction of herb-drug interaction effects.

This study addresses the pressing need for accurate stress detection systems by developing a machine learning-based framework that leverages two large-scale multimodal physiological datasets: NEURO and WESAD. The proposed approach utilizes both classical machine learning models and deep learning architectures to classify different types of stress. To effectively extract relevant features from time-series data, raw signals are converted into image representations using novel signal transformation techniques, including Gramian Angular Summation Fields (GAFs), Gramian Angular Difference Fields (GAFd), and Markov Transition Fields (MTF). Unlike conventional methods that focus on temporal transformations, our approach performs feature-axis transformations to capture spatial patterns in physiological signals. The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is employed to extract spatial features from these visual representations, significantly enhancing classification performance. Furthermore, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) visualizations identify the most influential input regions driving model decisions, providing insights into the physiological indicators of stress. Our results demonstrate strong generalization across both datasets, with feature-axis transformations based deep learning models achieving 98.3% accuracy and classical models like XGBoost and LightGBM reaching approximately 99% accuracy. The integration of Grad-CAM strengthens the model's interpretability by validating the relevance of learned features. Overall, this study showcases the potential of integrating advanced signal transformation techniques with explainable deep learning to develop robust and interpretable stress detection systems.

Abdominal organ segmentation in medical images remains a formidable challenge due to the intricate anatomy and low contrast present in these images. To overcome this, we introduce MSAE-U-Net, a novel deep learning architecture designed for accurate and robust abdominal segmentation. This model incorporates three pivotal innovations: the Multi-Scale Deformable Convolutional Block Attention Module (MS-D-CBAM), which facilitates multi-scale attention; the Adaptive Global Context Refinement ASPP (AGCR-ASPP), which dynamically refines context information; and the Rectangular Self-Calibration Module (RCM), which recalibrates spatial features for enhanced segmentation. These modules synergistically enhance both local detail and global context, leading to improved segmentation performance. Evaluations conducted on the Dresden Surgical Anatomy dataset yielded outstanding results for MSAE-U-Net, with a Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) of 93.13%, IoU of 92.01%, and precision of 94.67%. Notably, these outcomes surpass those of existing models such as U-Net, nnU-Net, and AbdomenNet. The superior performance of MSAE-U-Net underscores its potential for clinical applications, including preoperative planning and automated organ identification.

The diagnosis of brain tumors remains a critical challenge in oncology, with timely intervention crucial for improved patient outcomes. Recent advancements in medical imaging have led to the development of computer-aided diagnostic systems that leverage image classification techniques. This study proposes and evaluates five hybrid models for multi-class classification of brain tumors, combining the strengths of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with Support Vector Machines (SVM). Specifically, we integrate CNN architectures (VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, ResNet152, and EfficientNetB3) with LSTM layers to capture temporal dependencies in imaging data. The resulting outputs are then classified using SVM, which provides robust classification capabilities. Our methodology includes data augmentation to address class imbalance and increase the diversity of the training dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed models outperform current state-of-the-art approaches, with the ResNet152 + LSTM + SVM model achieving exceptional performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score) when optimized using the RMSprop optimizer. These findings have significant implications for the development of accurate and efficient computer-aided diagnostic systems for brain tumor classification.

The convergence of telecommunications and computer vision has revolutionized numerous applications in recent years. However, these two fields have traditionally operated independently, neglecting the potential synergies between their respective innovations. In challenging environments, such as those characterized by obstructions or limited lighting, integrating radio sensing and computer vision can significantly enhance problem-solving capabilities. Machine learning algorithms play a crucial role in fusing heterogeneous data from multiple sources, enabling the extraction of additional knowledge from raw sensor inputs.

This study introduces the CONVERGE paradigm, which seeks to integrate telecommunications and computer vision to create a unified framework for "View-to-Communicate, Communicate-to-View" applications. The proposed architecture is designed to support various use cases, including those in telecommunications, automotive, manufacturing, media, and healthcare. To facilitate research in these areas, CONVERGE provides an open experimental infrastructure that combines wireless communications and computer vision capabilities with a novel set of tools for data collection and analysis.

The results of this study demonstrate the feasibility of integrating telecommunications and computer vision using machine learning algorithms. The experimental infrastructure developed as part of this work enables the creation of large-scale datasets, which can be used to train and evaluate models in various application domains. This research has significant implications for the development of next-generation applications that require the fusion of multiple data sources and modalities.

This study integrates large-scale genetic and multi-omics datasets to elucidate the host's response to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). By leveraging the COVID-19 Host Genetics Initiative (HGI) Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS) data and publicly available transcriptomes and plasma proteomics, we construct an explainable artificial intelligence model that compares host infection mechanisms across these viral illnesses. Our analysis reveals shared antiviral pathways, such as type I interferon signaling, which are active in responses to all three viruses, alongside virus-specific mechanisms that distinguish COVID-19 from influenza and RSV. For instance, SARS-CoV-2 induces strong coagulation and renin-angiotensin system dysregulation, whereas influenza triggers robust T-cell activation, and RSV elicits an excessive neutrophil-driven inflammatory response. Genetic risk pathway fingerprints from GWAS highlight associations between COVID-19 severity and variants in interferon and inflammatory pathways, while host genetic effects in influenza point to distinct receptor usage with minimal overlap. Mendelian randomization identifies key causal proteins for COVID-19 severity, including ABO (blood group glycosyltransferase) and inflammatory mediators, suggesting that host glycomic and immune factors modulate disease outcomes. Our model integrates these multi-omic features to accurately distinguish COVID-19 from other viral infections, with SHAP interpretation confirming the predominance of the identified mechanisms in model predictions. This study provides a comprehensive comparative map of host responses in COVID-19 versus influenza and RSV, yielding biologically interpretable insights into both common antiviral defenses and unique pathogenic pathways that inform targeted therapies for severe respiratory viral diseases.

This study addresses critical challenges in 3D human pose estimation (HPE) and energy expenditure estimation (EEE), focusing on handling complex activities, improving generalization, and jointly enhancing both tasks within a unified framework. To address these challenges, we propose Pose2Met, an end-to-end framework that integrates HPE and EEE through a novel SpatioTemporal Aggregated Pose (STAP) representation. This representation is the core component of STAPFormer, a Transformer model designed for efficient and accurate motion modeling. By leveraging this representation, Pose2Met introduces a unified learning strategy that jointly optimizes pose dynamics and metabolic patterns within a single paradigm, enabling direct prediction of both 3D pose and energy expenditure from 2D pose inputs. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of Pose2Met in achieving performance comparable to traditional pipelines while significantly enhancing computational efficiency and robustness in practical applications. Experiments on Human3.6M and Vid2Burn-ADL datasets show that STAPFormer outperforms state-of-the-art models, with an MPJPE of 38.2 mm and a 22.1 kcal MAE for EEE. Under the unified learning framework, 2D pose-based EEE approaches the accuracy of 3D pose-based prediction, highlighting the potential of Pose2Met in intelligent fitness and healthcare applications.

This study addresses the challenges of sentiment analysis in short-form, noisy social media messages, where linguistic irregularities such as slang, emojis, and misspellings compromise model performance and interpretability. To mitigate these issues, a novel hybrid model is proposed that integrates a convolutional block with a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM) and a multi-head self-attention mechanism incorporating a noise-invariant contrastive head (NICH). This module normalizes noisy tokens before encoding, enabling the model to focus on sentiment-bearing tokens. The proposed approach is trained in two stages: an initial frozen phase followed by fine-tuning of word embeddings. Evaluation is conducted on two benchmark datasets: Sentiment140 (1.6M tweets) and IMDb (50k movie reviews). Results demonstrate that the hybrid model outperforms classical baselines, including logistic regression, support vector machines, and random forests, as well as an Attention-BiLSTM reference model. Notably, it achieves competitive performance with a compact Transformer-based baseline (DistilBERT), attaining 90.63% accuracy and 0.9079 F1 on Sentiment140 and 91.90% accuracy and 0.9203 F1 on IMDb. Furthermore, attention scores and a perturbation-based metric reveal that the model consistently focuses on sentiment-carrying tokens, providing valuable token-level explanations for real-time monitoring of customer feedback and brand perception in noisy social media streams.

Financial technology (Fin-tech) startups play a crucial role in driving innovation and disruption within the financial sector; however, their high failure rates underscore the need for sophisticated predictive tools to inform investment and policy decisions. To address this challenge, we develop a deep learning framework based on 1D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to categorize Fin-tech startup outcomes into four distinct categories: Initial Public Offering (IPO), Merger & Acquisition (M&A), Closure, and Unicorn status using a comprehensive dataset from Crunchbase. By leveraging the ability of CNNs to extract local and hierarchical patterns from structured scalar attributes such as team composition, funding velocity, investor diversity, and company age, our framework aims to improve predictive accuracy and mitigate class imbalance through the application of ADASYN oversampling. Our results demonstrate that XGBoost achieves state-of-the-art performance on this task, with 92.9% accuracy and 91.9% macro F1, while the proposed CNN model achieves a competitive 90.8% accuracy and 89.6% macro F1. Notably, our study highlights the trade-offs between tree-based models and deep learning approaches in multiclass startup outcome prediction, with implications for both research and practice in Fin-tech innovation and policy-making.

In the context of smart agricultural IoT systems, dynamic resource allocation poses significant challenges due to device heterogeneity and environmental variability. To address these issues, we present a novel framework that integrates edge computing capabilities from emerging 6G networks with reinforcement learning techniques for optimizing resource management. By leveraging the ultra-low latency and high bandwidth of 6G, our framework enables real-time communication and efficient task offloading, while reinforcement learning dynamically adapts to changing environment states and task requirements. A bespoke reward function is designed to balance competing objectives, including latency reduction, resource utilization, and energy efficiency. Experimental evaluations demonstrate substantial performance improvements, with a notable 35% decrease in latency and 25% increase in resource utilization compared to traditional approaches. Furthermore, our framework exhibits scalability under increasing device density and task complexity, underscoring its potential for applications such as precision irrigation, autonomous agriculture, and drone surveillance in smart agricultural settings.

The increasing availability of large datasets and advancements in artificial intelligence have led to the widespread adoption of large language models (LLMs) across various fields, including healthcare. The efficient utilization of extensive patient data and medical records has been shown to enhance diagnostic accuracy and enable disease-risk prediction. However, a comprehensive evaluation framework for assessing the application of LLMs in structured and unstructured medical data is lacking. This study proposes the HLLM-Potential Framework (Healthcare Large Language Model Potential Evaluation Framework), a systematic approach designed to evaluate the applicability and performance of LLMs in medical data analysis through comparative experiments with traditional models. Our methodology involves training existing LLMs on publicly available cardiovascular datasets, which encompass both structured and unstructured data, to perform disease prediction and health-risk assessment tasks. We also compare the predictive performance of various LLMs with traditional machine-learning and deep-learning models to quantify their differences. The results demonstrate that LLMs can effectively process structured and unstructured medical data, achieving accuracy and recall rates above 80% in cardiovascular disease prediction tasks. Furthermore, our experiments show that multimodal LLMs exhibit superior performance in ECG image classification tasks, with an overall accuracy rate of 85%. These findings suggest that LLMs offer notable advantages over traditional models, including high efficiency, flexibility, and multimodal integration capabilities, making them a promising tool for healthcare applications.

Sparse signal recovery is a fundamental problem in various fields, including medical imaging, remote sensing, machine learning, and data science. Many practical applications involve signals that are inherently non-negative due to the physical nature of measured quantities (e.g., pixel intensities, chemical concentrations, or power spectra). While there has been significant research on general sparse recovery and a growing body of work on non-negative sparse recovery, existing methods often treat these cases separately rather than providing a unified framework. This study proposes a novel sequential quadratic programming (SQP) framework that treats both general and non-negative sparse recovery as special instances of a single constrained optimization model, distinguished by the geometry of the trust region used to enforce feasibility. The framework leverages the Smoothed-ℓ0 approximation, which provides a smooth surrogate for the combinatorial ℓ0 norm. By exploiting the exact diagonal structure of the Hessian of the SL0 objective, we achieve accurate second-order search directions and superlinear convergence rates, outperforming first-order methods prevalent in the literature. For non-negative recovery, we incorporate interior-point ideas directly into the SQP subproblem, imposing an ellipsoidal trust region that keeps iterates strictly inside the non-negative orthant, ensuring stability and theoretical soundness. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and real-world tasks demonstrate that our unified framework outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms in terms of recovery accuracy, phase transition behavior, and robustness to noise.

Estimating heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) is a crucial task in many fields, particularly in healthcare and economics, where understanding the impact of interventions on diverse populations is essential for informed decision-making. However, existing methods often struggle with confounding variables, leading to biased estimates of treatment effects. To address this limitation, we introduce Bayesian causal forests with covariate balancing propensity scores (BCF-CBPS), a novel approach that integrates covariate balancing directly into the propensity score estimation step. By leveraging the strengths of both BCF and covariate balancing techniques, our method improves covariate balance and enables more accurate estimation of true treatment effect heterogeneity. Through extensive Monte Carlo simulations and real-world applications, we demonstrate that BCF-CBPS outperforms standard BCF and other machine learning methods for HTE estimation in terms of precision and robustness. The results highlight the potential of combining covariate balancing with the BCF model to provide reliable estimates of treatment effects in complex observational studies.

This study introduces a novel machine learning framework for the automated analysis and clustering of modern and contemporary Korean paintings based on image data. By integrating a pre-trained vision-language architecture with multi-layered feature extraction, our approach efficiently captures detailed formal characteristics, including color features from multiple color spaces and quantified texture information. The extracted feature vectors are then clustered using majority-label assignment, yielding an overall accuracy of 82.4%. This surpasses the performance of single-feature baselines (RGB: 82.0%, HSV: 81.3%, histogram: 51.0%, LBP: 68.8%, and GLCM: 73.7%). Notably, our method exhibits improved per-artist precision, enhanced boundary-case discrimination, and increased robustness for low-sample categories. The resulting artist clusters are characterized by unique color and texture profiles, as exemplified by representative images. Furthermore, we extend the analysis through automatic image captioning and zero-shot style assignment via matching image and text embeddings. Our findings demonstrate that machine learning-based image analysis offers a reliable and objective methodology for identifying and distinguishing visual characteristics of modern and contemporary Korean paintings, providing a quantitative approach to art-historical interpretation.

Landslide risk identification is a critical component of disaster relief operations and sustainable land use planning, as it enables the accurate mapping of susceptibility zones. However, traditional detection techniques often fall short due to their high workload, subjectivity, and inaccuracy, particularly when dealing with inaccessible landslide distributions. To address these limitations, this study proposes an explainable machine learning approach for landslide risk identification that integrates knowledge about probable landslide-causing factors.

The proposed method is based on a multistage fusion architecture, which improves the identification of different possible landslide types by selecting the most relevant features through an information gain filter-based feature selection technique. This is followed by training several linear and ensemble classification models using the filtered features. The resulting ensemble model demonstrates superior performance in landslide prediction tasks compared to other machine learning models. Furthermore, the proposed approach utilizes effective visualization techniques for accurate explainability, providing insights into the decision-making process.

The results of this study demonstrate the potential of the proposed method to improve landslide risk identification and provide valuable information for disaster relief operations and sustainable land use planning. The use of an explainable machine learning strategy addresses the limitations of traditional detection techniques, enabling more accurate and reliable landslide susceptibility mapping.

The proliferation of social networks has led to an unprecedented surge in text mining applications, with text analysis emerging as a pivotal technique for extracting valuable insights from online content. Social media platforms offer unparalleled access to vast amounts of text data, comprising user-generated comments that hold significant business value when analyzed. Sentiment analysis (SA), a subfield of natural language processing, aims to automatically identify and categorize opinions expressed in text as positive, negative, or neutral. This capability is particularly valuable for understanding public opinion, especially when applied to large-scale textual data from social media platforms.

However, the extraction of high-quality datasets from social media platforms is often hindered by platform-specific APIs, privacy constraints, and data usage limitations. To address this challenge, we propose TSAPM-BERT, a BERT-based framework that integrates a weighted attention mechanism with sentiment lexicon information and optimizes the learning rate to enhance aspect-level sentiment classification. We evaluate the performance of TSAPM-BERT on a benchmark emotion dataset, comparing its results against traditional machine learning and deep learning baselines.

Experimental results demonstrate that TSAPM-BERT achieves a training accuracy of 99.37% and testing accuracy of 98.52%, outperforming competing methods. This study provides a reproducible framework that bridges the gap between high-accuracy sentiment classification and the practical constraints of social media data usage, offering a valuable contribution to the field of text analysis and sentiment analysis.

This study presents a comprehensive image dataset designed for surface defect detection on waterborne painted wood products. The dataset comprises 13,400 high-resolution images, encompassing four distinct defect types: scratches, cracks, bubbles, and holes. Specifically, the dataset includes 3,645 bubble defects, 3,498 scratch defects, 3,256 crack defects, and 3,001 hole defects. These images were captured in an operational production facility in Jiangshan, China, utilizing specialized industrial cameras. To ensure data accuracy, professional annotators performed rigorous labeling of the images.

The primary objective of this research is to provide a critical resource for deploying deep learning models in real-world industrial assembly lines. By leveraging this dataset, researchers can develop and evaluate automated machine learning solutions for multi-class defect detection. Such techniques have the potential to significantly enhance defect detection and remediation processes, thereby ensuring finish integrity and final product quality. The dataset's release is expected to facilitate advancements in surface defect detection, ultimately contributing to improved manufacturing efficiency and product reliability.

Concept drift is a pervasive issue in machine learning-based systems, compromising the integrity and reliability of pre-trained models' predictions. To mitigate this challenge, effective drift detection and adaptation mechanisms are essential components in operational systems. Recent research has focused on deep learning-based methods, particularly autoencoders, due to their versatility in handling various tasks. Autoencoders learn data distributions and measure deviations using reconstruction loss; however, standard autoencoders may not always achieve optimal performance. This study investigates the optimization of autoencoder-based drift detection methods by exploring different types of autoencoders, including denoising, variational, and standard variants. A framework is proposed to select the best hyperparameters and parameters for each method using grid search and Bayesian optimization (BO). The framework is evaluated theoretically and empirically on eight datasets, comprising four synthetic and four real-world datasets. Results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in identifying optimal autoencoder configurations with improved drift detection performance, underscoring its potential to enhance the robustness of machine learning-based systems in dynamic environments.

This study addresses the challenge of utilizing social media data for urban and regional studies or public health monitoring by developing a classification framework to infer tweet locations with high precision. Given that only a small fraction (2%) of Twitter posts are geo-annotated, our approach relies solely on textual and contextual information within each post to predict its location. The proposed method offers worldwide coverage and adapts its spatial granularity according to the volume of posts in a given area, allowing for country-level predictions in low-activity regions and state-level predictions in high-activity zones. To evaluate the framework's performance, we conduct extensive experiments examining the impact of various factors on different regions around the world. Our analysis reveals that excluding tweets with limited location hints significantly enhances overall model quality, suggesting a trade-off between data inclusion and prediction accuracy.

The scarcity of data in certain engineering applications has been a long-standing challenge for machine learning methods. In tasks such as predictive maintenance, where faults are rare and expensive to obtain, the value of data is akin to gold. While synthetic data generation has gained significant attention in fields like computer vision and natural language processing, its application in time series analysis remains underexplored. Recent advancements in generative models have shown promise in augmenting time series data, but the use of denoising techniques, particularly diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs), warrants further investigation. This study aims to address this knowledge gap by developing and evaluating a novel methodology for data augmentation in time series classification and regression tasks. The proposed D3A-TS framework leverages DPMs to generate synthetic data, which is conditioned on meta-attributes to enhance its realism. An extensive evaluation using six diverse datasets demonstrates the efficacy of this approach in creating high-quality training data. Furthermore, an ablation study provides insight into the contribution of individual components to the overall performance. The results highlight the potential of D3A-TS as a valuable tool for augmenting time series data and improving the accuracy of machine learning models.

This study investigates the integration of machine learning and natural language processing techniques in film and television script creation, with a focus on script generation, plot construction, and character development. A deep neural network-based model is developed to generate scripts by leveraging a large dataset of existing film and television scripts. The model's performance is evaluated in terms of fluency, accuracy, and creativity, demonstrating promising results in short script generation. However, the model exhibits limitations in handling complex plots and nuanced emotional expressions, highlighting areas for future improvement. To address these challenges, ongoing research aims to enhance the model's capacity for intricate plot processing and emotional expression, while also optimizing computational efficiency and output quality. The findings of this study provide a foundational empirical basis for exploring the application of artificial intelligence in creative industries, offering new avenues for technological innovation and practical exploration in script creation.

This study addresses the pressing need for high-resolution soil moisture data at various depth horizons to improve our understanding of the Earth's energy and water cycles. Current products primarily focus on surface layers, leaving a significant gap in continuous datasets for deep soil horizons. To bridge this knowledge gap, we developed a global, daily multilayer soil moisture dataset (SWSM) spanning 2002-2021 using an advanced machine learning approach based on the XGBoost algorithm. The SWSM dataset provides spatially explicit estimates at a resolution of 0.05° for three distinct depth layers: 0-10 cm, 10-30 cm, and 30-60 cm. Through rigorous validation against in situ observations, our results demonstrate exceptional accuracy, with Pearson correlation coefficients surpassing 0.90 and root mean square errors below 0.05 across all depths. Furthermore, a feature importance analysis confirms the dataset's physical consistency, revealing depth-dependent patterns that align with established hydrological principles. The SWSM dataset offers valuable insights for various applications, including hydrologic modeling, agricultural water management, and climate change research, thanks to its comprehensive temporal coverage, fine spatial resolution, and multi-layer structure.

This study introduces ML-TWiX, a comprehensive dataset of monthly total water storage anomalies (TWSA) spanning from 1980 to 2012 on a global grid with a spatial resolution of 0.5° × 0.5°. The existing GRACE and GRACE Follow-On satellite missions have significantly advanced our understanding of TWSA, but their combined record is limited to approximately two decades, hindering long-term climate and hydrological research. To address this limitation, ML-TWiX leverages an ensemble of three machine learning models – Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gaussian Process Regression – trained on a diverse set of global hydrological and land surface model simulations. Each model was applied independently to reconstruct TWSA, and their outputs were subsequently combined through ensemble averaging, yielding a unified product with spatially explicit uncertainty estimates. The accuracy of ML-TWiX is validated against multiple independent datasets, including satellite laser ranging, storage deduced from the water mass balance closure, and global mean sea level budget estimates. This continuous reconstruction of global TWSA facilitates a wide range of applications in hydrology, climate science, and water resource assessment, thereby enhancing our capacity to investigate long-term climate variability and its impacts on global water resources.

The disparity between the skills imparted in educational institutions and those demanded by industry has become a pressing concern in today's fast-paced technological landscape. As industries continually evolve, they necessitate workers with up-to-date knowledge and novel competencies. This research endeavors to develop an adaptive framework that leverages machine learning to synchronize industry skill requirements with educational curricula, thereby generating personalized course recommendations.

To address this challenge, the proposed framework aggregates data from multiple sources, including job portals, industry reports, company career pages, and labor market analyses, to capture the current and emerging skill demands across various sectors. The developed Dynamic Termite Life Cycle Optimizer-driven Euclidean-Support Vector Machine (DTLC-Euclidean SVM) model predicts both present and future skill requirements in the job market by analyzing patterns in graduate skillsets and employer needs.

The framework's dynamic matching algorithm employs similarity metrics and k-Means Clustering techniques to map industry demands onto existing course content, while an automated course generation module suggests new or updated courses to address identified skill shortages. The framework's performance is evaluated using standard metrics, yielding accuracy (96.8%), precision (97.6%), recall (96.2%), and f1-score (97.3%) results that underscore its robustness and reliability in bridging the skill gap.

This data-driven approach enables continuous curriculum adaptation, fostering stronger alignment between academia and industry. Ultimately, the framework supports educators and policymakers in developing targeted educational programs that prepare students for real-world career opportunities, enhancing employability and addressing the demands of a rapidly evolving labor market.

The evaluation of Physical Education (PE) teaching effectiveness is a critical aspect of promoting students' physical development, engagement, and lifelong health performances. However, conventional assessment methods often suffer from subjectivity and fail to capture the complexities of PE instruction. This study aims to develop an integrated framework for evaluating PE teaching effectiveness using Multimodal Machine Learning (MML). A comprehensive dataset was compiled, incorporating instructional delivery (video/audio), physical movement patterns (via wearable sensors and motion capture), and student perceptions. To ensure data quality, two preprocessing techniques were employed: wavelet denoising to clean sensor signals and z-score normalization to control data variability across modalities. The Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) was utilized for feature extraction from video sources, capturing fine-grained movements and posture characteristics. A hybrid assessment framework, the Enhanced Marine Predators-driven Explainable Boosting Machine (EMP-EBM), was developed by combining the EMP's global optimization capabilities with the EBM's transparent structure. The proposed model demonstrated exceptional performance in identifying efficient PE teaching sessions, achieving accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score of 98.07%, 97.85%, 98.31%, and 98.07%, respectively. This research contributes to the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in education by providing an objective, adaptive, and multifaceted assessment framework for PE teaching effectiveness.

The development of reliable non-contact vital sign monitoring technologies for multiple patients remains a pressing challenge in healthcare settings. To address this issue, this study introduces a comprehensive radar-based dataset designed to facilitate the evaluation and improvement of simultaneous multi-patient vital sign monitoring methods. In collaboration with Ostrava University Training Hospital, we have created a dataset comprising recordings from two to three advanced medical simulation mannequins that mimic a broad spectrum of physiological states, including healthy resting conditions and acute emergencies such as apnea, cardiac arrest, and severe respiratory distress. The dataset encompasses over 100 uniquely configured sessions, with varied sensor geometries (top, frontal, lateral views at distances of 1-4 meters, and angles of 0°/45°) and radar parameters (ADC samples, chirp loops, ramp times, frame rates, and gains). Preliminary analysis using beamforming-based processing techniques has demonstrated the dataset's potential for developing advanced signal processing and machine learning pipelines. The results show mean heart-rate and breathing-rate errors near clinical thresholds (MAE ≈ 6.6 bpm for HR, 1.47 bpm for RR), underscoring the utility of this dataset in advancing non-invasive multi-patient monitoring systems. In accordance with FAIR principles, all data are thoroughly documented and publicly accessible, supporting reproducible research efforts toward the development of reliable non-contact vital sign monitoring technologies.

In elderly cardiac surgery patients, intraoperative acquired pressure injury (IAPI) poses a significant risk due to compromised skin integrity and impaired perfusion. Reliable prediction of IAPI risk is crucial for prevention and personalized care. To address this challenge, we developed machine learning models using data from 12,222 patients aged 60 years or older in the MIMIC-IV database. Our approach involved training seven classifiers on 24 clinically relevant features and integrating them via a stacking ensemble method. Internal evaluation employed stratified fivefold cross-validation with 10 repetitions and bootstrapped confidence intervals (2,000 resamples) to quantify uncertainty from missing data. Multiple imputations (m = 2000) were used to account for missing values. External validation was performed on an independent dataset of 355 patients from Nanjing First Hospital, with class imbalance addressed through resampling. Calibration metrics and SHAP analysis with bootstrapping were employed to assess model reliability and interpretability. The ensemble model demonstrated strong discrimination in both internal (AUC = 0.857) and external (AUC = 0.783) validation, exhibiting stable performance across imputations (mean AUC: 0.997 ± 0.004). Calibration analysis confirmed good agreement between predicted and observed risks. Key predictors, including atrial fibrillation, lymphocyte count, and operative time, were clinically plausible and robustly identified. This study presents an externally validated ensemble learning framework for early IAPI risk prediction in elderly cardiac surgery patients, offering improved methodological transparency and a clinically actionable tool for perioperative risk stratification and prevention.

The escalating prevalence of diabetes worldwide necessitates the development of accurate and timely predictive models to mitigate its severe consequences. Machine learning regression techniques have revolutionized diabetes prediction by elucidating intricate relationships between risk factors and disease progression, thereby enabling more precise and personalized interventions. Recent advancements in feature selection, regularization, and ensemble methods have significantly enhanced the predictive performance of these algorithms. However, the proliferation of diverse datasets, evaluation metrics, and regression techniques has created a pressing need for standardized frameworks to ensure fair and reproducible comparisons. This study aims to address this challenge by conducting an in-depth analysis of current trends in diabetes prediction using regression models. Specifically, we investigate the evolution and performance of machine learning techniques, develop a reference framework for evaluating and benchmarking diabetes prediction models, assess the effectiveness of leading regression algorithms, and outline future research directions that prioritize robust, explainable, and patient-centric prediction models capable of seamless integration into clinical practice.

Alzheimer's Disease (AD), the leading cause of dementia, necessitates early diagnosis to facilitate effective intervention. To address this critical need, we propose a hybrid framework that combines SqueezeNet, a lightweight convolutional neural network, with ensemble stacking of machine learning models for enhanced AD detection using MRI scans. Leveraging a large dataset comprising 3,382 images across four classes (Mild, Moderate, Very Mild Dementia, and Non-Dementia), our approach extracts efficient features from SqueezeNet while minimizing computational demands without compromising diagnostic accuracy. A stacked ensemble is employed to integrate predictions from diverse models, including Multi-Layer Perceptrons with varying architectures, XGBoost, Cat Boost, and AdaBoost, thereby optimizing robustness and addressing class imbalance through rigorous validation. Our proposed model achieves state-of-the-art performance, boasting an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 94.9% and accuracy of 89%, outperforming individual models and demonstrating superior reliability in clinical environments with limited resources. The framework's scalability, reliability, and cost-effectiveness make it a promising tool for real-time AD detection and classification in clinical settings.

The proliferation of high-dimensional feature spaces in malware classification has hindered the performance of machine learning models. To mitigate this issue, we investigate the efficacy of four dimensionality-reduction techniques – Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), and Autoencoder-based reduction – applied to opcode-frequency representations of malware. Our study utilizes a comprehensive dataset comprising 82,569 samples and 1796 opcodes, allowing us to examine the impact of each reduction method across multiple target dimensions and two classifier architectures: Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and a three-layer Multilayer Perceptron (MLP). The results indicate that LDA achieves superior separability at lower dimensions, while PCA excels at higher dimensions where variance preservation is crucial. In contrast, Autoencoder-based reduction consistently yields high accuracy with compact representations, whereas UMAP exhibits limited benefits for tabular opcode data. Our findings underscore the trade-offs between linear and non-linear reduction strategies, providing valuable insights for selecting efficient feature compression methods in large-scale malware analysis.

This study addresses the challenge of estimating twin zygosity using self-reported physical similarity features as a cost-effective alternative to genetic testing. A machine learning framework was developed and trained on data from the Iranian School-aged Twin Registry (2018-2021), comprising 5,077 twin pairs with 11 physical similarity traits. To mitigate class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was employed. Eight machine learning algorithms were evaluated, including K-nearest neighbors, support vector machine, logistic regression, random forest, decision tree, and boosting techniques such as XGBoost and Gradient Boosting Classifier. The performance of each model was assessed using multiple metrics after hyperparameter optimization via GridSearchCV. Results indicate that XGBoost achieved superior accuracy (85.57%) and F1-score (86.72%), outperforming other algorithms. SHAP analysis revealed the most influential physical traits in zygosity prediction, further substantiating the efficacy of XGBoost with SMOTE oversampling as an optimal framework for twin zygosity estimation through self-reported questionnaires. The study's findings have implications for reducing costs associated with twin research and provide a scalable alternative to genetic testing. Future studies will involve external validation and comparison of predicted results with genetic test outcomes, contingent upon sufficient funding.

In the field of epilepsy diagnosis, accurate recognition of seizures from electroencephalogram (EEG) data is crucial for timely intervention and informed decision-making. However, traditional centralized machine learning approaches pose significant privacy risks by necessitating the sharing of sensitive patient information across institutions. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that leverages federated and explainable machine learning to support epileptic seizure recognition while preserving data confidentiality.

Our study employs a publicly available EEG-derived dataset comprising five original classes, which are reformulated into a binary classification task distinguishing between seizure and non-seizure events. Data partitioning at the patient level is implemented to prevent information leakage, with separate training and testing sets established. A simulated federated learning environment is developed, incorporating multiple clients with both independent and non-independent data distributions, and executed over fifty communication rounds using the Federated Averaging strategy.

We evaluate the performance of various machine learning models, including Support Vector Machine, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and K Nearest Neighbors, using standardized hyperparameter configurations. Model efficacy is assessed through accuracy, area under the ROC curve, confusion matrices, and learning curves. To provide transparency into model decision-making, explainability techniques such as SHAP and LIME are applied.

The results demonstrate that federated learning can achieve competitive performance while safeguarding data privacy, and that explainable methods offer valuable insights into seizure classification decisions. This study serves as a proof-of-concept evaluation using an EEG-derived dataset, highlighting the methodological feasibility of this approach rather than its clinical deployment potential.

The proliferation of digital documents has led to an increased reliance on tables as a fundamental format for representing structured data across various domains, including healthcare, education, and finance. Table recognition is a critical task aimed at automatically converting table content from images into editable and machine-readable formats, thereby enhancing processing efficiency and accuracy. Despite significant advancements in convolutional neural network (CNN)-based and Transformer-based approaches, existing methods continue to face challenges in handling complex table layouts, diverse formats, and high computational costs. To address these limitations, we introduce the Spatial Pyramid Pooling-enhanced Multi-modal Linear Transformer (SPPLTR), a novel table-recognition model that integrates spatial pyramid pooling with a causal linear-Transformer framework. SPPLTR effectively captures both structural and textual information in table images through a spatial pyramid context fusion module and a causal linear Transformer, thereby improving the understanding of table structures. Furthermore, the model incorporates a multi-head causal linear attention mechanism with automatic mixed precision support, enabling more efficient and stable inference. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the PubTabNet dataset demonstrate that SPPLTR achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing most existing models across various evaluation metrics. The proposed model presents a promising solution for table recognition tasks and paves the way for further advancements in structured document understanding.

Wind turbine systems operating in cold climates are susceptible to icing events, which can compromise structural integrity if undetected. To address this critical issue, we have developed an intelligent information fusion framework that combines vibration-based structural dynamics with environmental data through a pipeline of automated Operational Modal Analysis (OMA) and temperature compensation. This study evaluates the performance of five classification architectures—Optimized Threshold (OT), Random Forest (RF), XGBoost (XGB), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)—using high-fidelity experimental data from a large-scale climate chamber, processed through rolling-window time-series cross-validation. To ensure the reliability of our framework in safety-critical applications, we prioritize Recall to minimize False Negative errors and incorporate non-parametric statistical validation techniques, including Kruskal-Wallis tests and Dunn’s post-hoc analysis, to quantify model stability. Our results demonstrate a significant performance gap between physics-informed models and advanced ensemble and deep learning methods, with the OT model achieving an exceptional operational reliability of 96.00%. This finding highlights the importance of meticulous feature-level fusion and domain-specific engineering in enhancing system resilience, rather than relying on increased algorithmic complexity. The proposed methodology provides a scalable foundation for multi-sensor fusion and adaptive predictive maintenance in renewable energy infrastructures, offering a promising solution to ensure the safe and reliable operation of wind turbine systems in cold climates.

Here's an original abstract for the machine learning study:

The proliferation of deepfake content on social media platforms poses significant challenges to maintaining trust and preventing misinformation. As a result, detecting machine-generated textual content is crucial in mitigating its adverse effects. This research aims to develop an intelligent framework for identifying short texts (e.g., tweets) as either human-generated or bot-created. The proposed framework leverages the DeBERTaV3 model, coupled with the TweepFake and PHEME datasets, to analyze social media posts and determine their origin. In contrast to existing approaches, this study evaluates the efficacy of various deep learning frameworks, including BERT, RoBERTa, SVM-RBF, random forest, SVM, CNN, and LSTM, in detecting fake news. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves an accuracy rate of 97.12%, outperforming other models in terms of efficiency and effectiveness. The study's findings highlight the significance of data preprocessing techniques and embedding methods in facilitating accurate tweet classification, ultimately enabling the identification of machine-generated content on social media platforms.

This study investigates the efficacy of machine learning-based credit-scoring models relative to traditional statistical methods using a real-world dataset comprising 1000 loan applications. The primary objective is to assess whether AI-driven approaches can offer superior predictive accuracy, interpretability, and stability in the face of macroeconomic downturns compared to conventional techniques. Four model variants are developed: logistic regression and decision trees as examples of classical models, and XGBoost gradient-boosting ensemble and multilayer perceptron neural networks as alternatives based on AI. The R programming environment (v4.3.1) is employed to implement a 70:30 train-test split, inner cross-validation, and harmony spatial search for hyperparameter tuning. Results indicate that the XGBoost model achieves optimal performance with a cumulative gain curve of 0.89 and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.89, outperforming the neural network (CGC: 0.87; AUC: 0.87) and logistic regression (CGC: 0.76; AUC: 0.76). SHAP analysis reveals that credit amount, loan duration, and age are key predictors of creditworthiness. Notably, XGBoost exhibits stability in predictions during stress-testing (AUC = 0.83), whereas logistic regression performance declines significantly (AUC = 0.68). The findings support the notion that AI-powered tools possess superior predictive capabilities and interpretability, rendering them viable alternatives to traditional methods in modern financial institutions.

The accurate assignment of imaging protocols is a crucial aspect of radiology workflows, requiring precise and efficient classification of complex medical images. This study investigates the capabilities of GPT-4 in text-based imaging protocol classification, comparing its performance to that of human experts and established fine-tuned models. We evaluated GPT-4 alongside a fine-tuned BERT model and a fine-tuned LLaMA-3 model on clinical indications corresponding to 11 head MRI protocol categories. Model predictions were benchmarked against expert-validated ground truth labels using F1 scores, with each model's reasoning coherence reviewed by a board-certified radiologist. Notably, GPT-4 achieved an F1 score of 0.83, which while lower than the 0.91 and 0.93 obtained by BERT and LLaMA-3 respectively, was surpassed only by human experts with a score of 0.96. However, GPT-4 consistently produced highly interpretable explanations that demonstrated a nuanced understanding of clinical language and imaging rationale. Furthermore, when prompted by the output of a fine-tuned classifier, specifically LLaMA-3, GPT-4's accuracy improved substantially, suggesting that structured collaboration between general and specialized models can enhance both performance and transparency in decision-support workflows. These findings indicate that GPT-4 has potential as an interpretable tool for supporting clinical decision-making when used appropriately.

The molecular underpinnings of clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), an aggressive malignancy characterized by single-nucleotide variants in VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2, have been extensively studied. Nevertheless, the contributions of structural variation (SV) and three-dimensional genome architecture to ccRCC pathogenesis remain poorly understood. To address this knowledge gap, we conducted a comprehensive multi-omics analysis to elucidate the role of SV in driving oncogenic dysregulation during ccRCC progression.

Our investigation revealed novel associations between SV and oncogenic targets, as well as multidimensional reorganization of three-dimensional genome architecture. Notably, we identified an uncharacterized pathogenic enhancer hijacking event that leads to constitutive activation of the proto-oncogene SEMA5B, which was experimentally validated through functional assays. To further translate these findings into clinical practice, we developed a machine learning-based prognostic framework using enhancer hijacking signatures.

The results of our study provide new insights into the complex interplay between SV and three-dimensional chromatin architecture in driving ccRCC oncogenesis. Moreover, the development of a prognostic tool based on enhancer hijacking signatures offers a valuable resource for clinicians seeking to improve patient outcomes through more accurate risk stratification and personalized treatment strategies.

The global prevalence of anemia is staggering, affecting over one billion individuals worldwide. This debilitating condition arises from insufficient hemoglobin levels due to iron or vitamin deficiencies, blood loss, or genetic disorders such as sickle cell disease and thalassemia. The diagnosis of anemia poses significant challenges in resource-constrained settings, particularly rural areas, where costly blood tests, limited medical personnel, and inadequate infrastructure hinder timely intervention.

To address these limitations, machine learning models have been explored for their potential to analyze blood count records and images from various bodily sites, including the eye conjunctiva, lip mucosa, fingernails, palms, and blood smears. This study undertakes a comprehensive review of artificial intelligence techniques applied to the diagnosis of three distinct types of anemia: iron deficiency, sickle cell disease, and thalassemia. A comparative analysis is conducted on machine learning, deep learning, optimization algorithms, hybrid approaches, and other methods in terms of accuracy, key features, research gaps, and future prospects.

Furthermore, this investigation examines the correlation between dataset sizes and model accuracy to inform best practices for anemia diagnosis using artificial intelligence. The integration of AI into real-time anemia diagnosis offers a promising solution, providing non-invasive, quick, affordable, and accurate diagnoses with explainable AI techniques that enhance physician trust and usability in remote areas. Although traditional invasive tests remain the gold standard for accurate anemia diagnosis, incorporating AI can significantly reduce the burden on healthcare professionals and facilitate diagnostic confirmation.

The Iraqi construction industry plays a pivotal role in driving economic growth within the country. However, managing housing projects efficiently remains a significant challenge due to uncertainties surrounding their productivity. This study investigates the application of machine learning techniques for predicting the implementation productivity of housing projects in Iraq based on project design and execution characteristics. Three advanced predictive models were developed and evaluated: artificial neural networks (ANN), extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), and support vector regression (SVR). A dataset comprising 67 actual housing projects was utilized, featuring seven input variables and one output variable representing implementation productivity. The evaluation results indicate that the XGBoost model demonstrated exceptional predictive accuracy with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.998 and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 0.8%. In comparison, the ANN model also exhibited strong performance (R2 = 0.92 and MAPE = 4.1%). Conversely, the SVR model showed relatively lower accuracy with an R2 value of 0.813 and MAPE of 6.6%. Feature importance analysis revealed that built-up area was the most influential factor in productivity, followed by quantities of brick and concrete works. The findings suggest that machine learning techniques, particularly XGBoost and ANN, can serve as effective tools for supporting decision-making during the early stages of housing project planning by predicting executive performance based on project data.

Advances in machine learning and computational methods have the potential to significantly accelerate materials discovery by enabling accurate predictions of material properties at reduced costs. However, a major bottleneck remains in providing high-quality input data to algorithms and optimizing model parameters. High-entropy materials, a distinct class of valuable materials, hold promise for overcoming limitations in conventional battery materials. By leveraging learning algorithms and quantum computing, researchers can design and fabricate novel materials for batteries and supercapacitors before chemical synthesis occurs. Our study focuses on the intersection of quantum computing and high-entropy material structure, particularly high-entropy MXenes. We examine strategies for encoding and decoding crystal structures, a crucial aspect in developing comprehensive databases for high-entropy materials. Furthermore, we investigate the application of deep learning algorithms to facilitate material discovery prior to synthesis, as well as their potential for identifying suitable high-entropy materials for energy storage applications. Ultimately, our research explores the emerging opportunities presented by quantum computing and artificial intelligence approaches for determining high-entropy material structures in the context of energy-related fields.

This study proposes an integrated artificial intelligence-driven framework to enhance Management Information Systems (MIS) capabilities for analyzing sustainable supply chains and environmental impact. To address three core tasks, research employs a combination of classical machine learning regression models, including Linear Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and SVR, alongside a deep learning-based Gated TabTransformer (GTT). Additionally, a Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE) is utilized for unsupervised anomaly detection, while a hybrid interpretability model constructed with Explainable Boosting Machines (EBM) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) provides explainable insights. The results demonstrate that Linear Regression and ensemble models exhibit superior performance in terms of accuracy and robustness, outperforming existing methods. Notably, the Gated TabTransformer achieves strong generalization capabilities, while the Variational Graph Autoencoder detects anomalies by learning topological and feature-level deviations. Furthermore, the framework enables interpretable local and global insights through EBM-SHAP, facilitating data-driven decision making with transparency.
