This comprehensive solution bridges sustainability, artificial intelligence, and MIS, advancing the state-of-the-art in sustainability analytics for supply chains.

The increasing adoption of Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) in military and civilian applications has created a pressing need for robust security measures to mitigate the risk of routing attacks. These networks, comprised of numerous small, low-cost devices, are inherently vulnerable to various types of assaults when deployed in exposed and unsecured environments. The susceptibility of WSNs to attacks such as wormhole, sinkhole, sybil, and black hole attacks poses significant security threats, including exploitation of sensor node vulnerabilities and wireless network unreliability.

This research aims to develop an effective Intrusion Detection System (IDS) for cluster-based WSNs, where a Cluster Head (CH) is selected within each cluster and a Group Head (GH) oversees the behavior of nodes and networks. A novel Dual Level Node Authentication Model with Node Behaviour Analysis (DLNA-NBA) is proposed, incorporating machine learning techniques to enhance intrusion detection accuracy.

The results demonstrate that DLNA-NBA significantly improves intrusion detection capabilities, achieving 98.9% accuracy in dual-level node authentication and 99.2% accuracy in intrusion detection. The proposed model's improved node verification, optimized feature utilization, and efficient behavioral pattern analysis ensure secure communication and reduced false alarms in WSN environments. Overall, this research contributes to the development of more secure and reliable WSNs by providing a robust IDS that can effectively detect and prevent routing attacks.

This study investigates the application of machine learning (ML) models for predicting sports-related injuries in professional football players. A dataset comprising 300 male athletes aged 18-28, monitored over two competitive seasons (2021-2022), was analyzed to identify factors contributing to injury risk. Injuries were defined as musculoskeletal conditions causing at least one missed training session or match, confirmed via ICD-10 diagnoses. Daily data on training workload, recovery, wellness, heart-rate variability, cumulative minutes played, and injury history were collected and preprocessed using normalization, one-hot encoding, and feature selection techniques.

The study employed 10-fold cross-validation to train logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest models, evaluating their accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). Results indicated that random forests outperformed other models, achieving an average accuracy of 85.6% ± 2.1%, precision of 82.1% ± 1.9%, recall of 80.3% ± 2.4%, F1-score of 81.2% ± 2.2%, and AUC of 90.5% ± 1.6%. Explainable AI techniques, including SHAP and LIME, were applied to identify the strongest predictors of injury risk, highlighting prior injury, training intensity, and recovery time as key factors.

The findings demonstrate that ensemble ML methods can provide robust, interpretable, and actionable insights for injury prevention in professional football players. These results support data-driven strategies to optimize training and reduce injury incidence. Future research should aim to validate the predictive models across multiple sports and integrate additional physiological and genetic factors to enhance their accuracy and generalizability.

The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into precision medicine has revolutionized healthcare by enabling more personalized treatment approaches. However, the economic viability of this paradigm shift remains a subject of debate. This study aimed to investigate the cost-effectiveness of AI-powered precision medicine through a comprehensive review and analysis of existing economic evaluations.

A systematic search of five major databases was conducted to identify relevant studies, with data extraction and risk-of-bias assessment performed using the ECOBIAS checklist. The findings suggest that AI-PM is cost-saving or cost-effective in approximately 89% of base-case analyses, with incremental cost-effectiveness ratios ranging from dominant to $129,174 per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY). Notably, the interquartile ranges of incremental costs and QALY gains indicate modest health benefits at minimal additional expenses.

The results also highlight significant value heterogeneity, with modeling choices and system-level factors emerging as key sources of uncertainty in estimated net monetary benefits. Furthermore, this study reveals that AI-PM adoption is hindered by low adaptability and underreported key value factors, underscoring the need for further research to address these knowledge gaps. Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the economic feasibility of AI-powered precision medicine, with implications for healthcare policymakers and stakeholders seeking to optimize resource allocation.

Here is a rewritten abstract for the machine learning study:

This study addresses the pressing need to improve postoperative sepsis prediction in patients undergoing upper urinary tract stone surgery. Sepsis remains a leading cause of perioperative mortality, necessitating the development of accurate predictive models. This research aims to integrate laboratory parameters using machine learning (ML) and Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) algorithm to create a simple yet effective model for postoperative sepsis prediction. A comprehensive dataset comprising 7,464 patients was analyzed, including 155 pre- and postoperative features. Key variables were selected through Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) regression and correlation analysis, which facilitated the development of eight machine learning algorithms. The model's performance was rigorously evaluated using Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, learning curve, calibration plot, and decision curve. The SHAP algorithm was employed to analyze variable importance, providing transparent insights into the key drivers of urosepsis. Notably, 116 out of 155 variables differed significantly between the urosepsis and non-urosepsis groups, with eight predictive variables identified through LASSO regression: postoperative IL-6, SAA, PCT/ALB, NLPR, PT, ALB, HCT, and neutrophil. The Light Gradient Boosting Machine achieved superior performance, with AUCs of 1.0, 0.90, and 0.88 for the training, validation, and test cohorts, respectively. The model's robustness was confirmed by a good fit, strong calibration, and positive clinical utility. Postoperative PCT/ALB, neutrophil, IL-6, ALB, and PT were identified as key predictors through SHAP analysis. A publicly accessible web application was developed to facilitate both patients and physicians in early risk stratification and personalized intervention. This study demonstrates the successful development and validation of a robust, interpretable model based on postoperative laboratory biomarkers, offering a practical tool for sepsis prediction and prevention in clinical practice.

In recent years, the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has become increasingly mandatory, leading to a significant increase in psychological and emotional strain among students. Traditional models of technology adoption often fail to account for the challenges posed by compulsory digital environments, where design flaws can result in cognitive overload and student resistance. This study aims to address this gap by developing and validating a student-centered framework that examines the impact of AI system design on negative technological experiences. Specifically, we investigate how system reliability, task routinization, and information richness influence students' perceptions of AI systems.

A quantitative research design was employed, with data collected from 229 undergraduate students across Malaysian universities. Exploratory factor analysis, correlation analysis, and multiple regression were used to examine relationships among key constructs and validate the hypothesized model. Our findings indicate that system reliability and information richness are significant predictors of reduced negative technological experiences, whereas task routinization has no such effect. Notably, we found that negative technological experience serves as a mediator, amplifying techno-resistance and decreasing academic performance.

The results of this study have important implications for the design of AI educational technologies in compulsory settings. By reconceptualizing techno-resistance as an adaptive response to cognitive and emotional strain, our research advances theoretical understanding by integrating digital dissonance with sociotechnical and cognitive load perspectives. The findings highlight the need for more student-aligned AI systems that prioritize system reliability, information richness, and user-centered design principles to mitigate negative technological experiences and promote improved educational outcomes.

Here is the rewritten abstract:

The advent of quantum computing has sparked interest in its potential to simulate complex quantum systems more efficiently than classical computers. However, recent studies have demonstrated that machine learning algorithms can effectively predict various properties of quantum systems using only classical data. Despite theoretical proposals for tasks where quantum advantages are evident, these examples often rely on cryptographic functions and do not reflect real-world laboratory scenarios. This paper addresses this gap by establishing a provable quantum advantage in learning quantum observables from measured-out classical data. We investigate two types of observables: first, we demonstrate a learning advantage for linear combinations of Pauli strings; subsequently, we extend our findings to a broader class of unitarily parametrized observables. For each case, we identify the boundaries between tasks that can be efficiently learned classically and those requiring quantum resources. Our classical hardness results rely on the assumption that BQP-hard processes cannot be simulated by polynomial-size classical circuits, and we also provide an efficient quantum learning algorithm. These findings clarify when quantum computing offers practical benefits for learning problems in quantum many-body physics and suggest potential directions for future research into quantum learning improvements.

Deep learning has witnessed significant advancements with the emergence of generative adversarial networks (GANs) as a pivotal framework for generating synthetic data. GANs have been widely adopted across various applications, including high-resolution image synthesis, speech generation, medical data reconstruction, educational data simulation, and style transfer. Despite their success in improving quality and diversity of generated data, challenges persist in convergence instability, mode collapse, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and evaluating the quality of synthetic data.

This study aims to provide an in-depth analysis of 45 distinct GAN architectures by categorizing them based on learning paradigm, application domain, network design, associated datasets, and open challenges. By examining the evolution of these architectures, this research seeks to outline prospective avenues for future research, including the integration of GANs with reinforcement learning, attention-based architectures, and large language models (LLMs). The framework presented in this study offers a comprehensive perspective on current trends and opportunities in the GAN landscape, providing researchers and practitioners with a coherent understanding of the field's trajectory.

Urban recommender systems are increasingly pivotal in shaping equitable, efficient, and citizen-centric urban services as cities integrate pervasive sensing technologies, data platforms, and artificial intelligence. This comprehensive review synthesizes a vast body of peer-reviewed research across six key domains: mobility, healthcare, energy, tourism, retail, and e-governance. Our analysis reveals that collaborative filtering, content-based methods, hybrid designs, deep learning, and context-aware approaches are being increasingly linked to urban decision-making needs through the development of novel taxonomies.

The review highlights the uneven distribution of advanced techniques such as deep and graph-based models across governance and energy domains, with most empirical studies concentrated in mobility and tourism. We examine how heterogeneous data sources, including IoT streams, geospatial signals, environmental indicators, and demographic attributes, are being integrated to support personalized recommendations under constraints such as latency, reliability, and privacy.

Notably, our review identifies recent advances that address sparsity and cold start through the use of graph neural models, sequence modeling, and transfer learning. Additionally, we cover operational enablers such as edge inference and streaming architectures for real-time recommendation. The survey also assesses risk and governance dimensions, including privacy preservation, fairness, exposure balance across neighborhoods, explainability, and mechanisms for audit and oversight.

Our analysis reveals opportunities in pollution management, citizen education, and participatory platforms that broaden civic engagement. Furthermore, we outline how emerging technologies such as hybrid physical-virtual interactions, digital twins, immersive interfaces, generative models, and quantum algorithms may reshape personalization and oversight in city-scale settings. Finally, we emphasize the need for field evaluations and standardized benchmarks to jointly measure accuracy, latency, robustness to distribution shift, and equity.

This study investigates the cellular impacts of genetic variants associated with Alzheimer's disease (AD). Genome-wide association studies have implicated immune and metabolic pathways in AD, but their specific roles remain unclear. To address this knowledge gap, we employed bidirectional two-sample Mendelian randomization to identify single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-mediated immunometabolic signatures in AD patients. Our analysis revealed both positively and negatively correlated immune cell types and metabolic pathways, which were further refined through integrated single-cell omics analysis of patient brains and peripheral blood samples.

The results showed distinct astrocyte subpopulations in patient brains with varying associations with AD, as well as predominantly AD-negative metabolic signatures in peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs). Leveraging these findings, we developed a blood transcriptome-based AD prediction model using machine learning approaches. The optimized model is applicable to both European and Asian populations, enabling pre-symptomatic detection of familial AD and effective discrimination from other neurodegenerative disorders. Our study provides novel evidence for the critical role of immunometabolism in AD and offers a practical predictive tool suitable for large-scale population screening.

Machine Learning Models for Predicting Surgical Correction Dosage in Horizontal Non-Paretic Strabismus

The variability of surgical outcomes in strabismus surgery remains a pressing concern, underscoring the need for more accurate and reliable methods to predict dosage. This study investigates the application of machine learning models to forecast dosages based on preoperative features, with a focus on explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to determine feature importance. A retrospective analysis of 767 patients who underwent strabismus surgery between 2003 and 2022 at the University Clinic of Ulm was conducted, resulting in a streamlined dataset for model development. Using a multilayer perceptron implemented with the torch neural network library, we evaluated the performance of various models, including the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno algorithm. The results indicate that this algorithm achieved the highest number of predictions within an acceptable range, with root mean square errors (RMSE) of 0.54 mm and 0.71 mm for labels L1 and L2, respectively. Notably, feature permutation and integrated gradients analysis revealed the preoperative angle of deviation as the most critical factor in dosage prediction, followed by near angle of deviation, simultaneous cover test, Hirschberg test angle, and refraction of own glasses used. The findings suggest that machine learning models, particularly those incorporating XAI techniques, hold promise for improving surgical outcome predictions in strabismus surgery. Future work will focus on expanding the dataset to enhance prediction accuracy.

The proliferation of fake news on social media poses a significant threat to civic trust and public safety, with emotionally charged content exacerbating its spread through user interactions. To address this challenge, we aim to develop an effective framework for detecting anomalous propagation subgraphs indicative of coordinated misinformation campaigns. This study proposes the Multi-Graph Neural Network Attention-based Propagation Learning for Emotion-Aware Anomaly Detection (MAPLE) framework, which integrates sentiment features from users' historical posts with network structures through a novel attention-based fusion mechanism. By combining multiple Graph Neural Networks (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE), MAPLE leverages the strengths of each model to improve anomaly detection performance. Theoretical analyses demonstrate that MAPLE preserves mutual information, reduces variance, amplifies anomaly margins, and maximizes entropy. Empirical evaluations on the PolitiFact and GossipCop datasets reveal that MAPLE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving F1-score improvements of 43% and 2.17%, respectively, while maintaining robustness across datasets. The proposed framework provides a unified multi-Graph Neural Network fusion approach with theoretical underpinnings for subgraph anomaly detection in the context of emotionally charged content.

High-Dimensional Feature Selection via Dynamic Binary Optimization

The proliferation of high-dimensional datasets in machine learning has underscored the need for efficient feature selection techniques to mitigate computational complexity and enhance classifier generalization. Traditional binary metaheuristics often rely on static control parameters, which can lead to suboptimal performance due to premature convergence and poor adaptability. To address these limitations, this study introduces the Dynamic Binary Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm (DBSMOA), a novel nature-inspired binary optimizer that leverages the foraging dynamics of swordfish to navigate complex search spaces.

The proposed algorithm extends the original Swordfish Movement Optimization Algorithm by incorporating dynamic behavioral adaptation mechanisms, enabling a self-adjusting balance between exploration and exploitation throughout the optimization process. DBSMOA employs a sigmoid-based probabilistic binary mapping and dynamically reassesses agent roles through a performance-driven elitist strategy to maintain diversity and stability during search.

Comprehensive evaluations on 52 benchmark datasets demonstrate that DBSMOA achieves superior performance compared to twelve state-of-the-art binary optimizers, including Binary Particle Swarm Optimization (bPSO), Binary Genetic Algorithm (bGA), and Binary Grey Wolf Optimizer (bGWO). The results indicate that DBSMOA delivers competitive accuracy, compact feature subsets, and strong computational efficiency, highlighting its robustness, scalability, and suitability for high-dimensional feature selection and other binary optimization tasks.

This study addresses the pressing need for non-invasive and accessible screening methods for high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN2/3), a precursor to cervical cancer, particularly in resource-limited settings. Current screening approaches, such as HPV testing and colposcopy, are limited by their invasiveness and accessibility issues. To mitigate these challenges, we developed a machine learning-based model that leverages routine blood biomarkers to assess the risk of high-grade CIN.

Our approach involved collecting data from 248 patients, comprising 128 with high-grade CIN (CIN2/3) and 120 with low-grade CIN (CIN1), who underwent comprehensive clinical evaluations. A total of 29 clinical characteristics and blood test measurements were considered for model development. We employed four feature selection algorithms to identify key predictors and trained 11 machine learning models using a dataset split into training (70%) and testing (30%) cohorts.

Model performance was evaluated using multiple metrics, including learning curves, receiver operating characteristic curves (ROC), area under the curve (AUC), Brier score, calibration curves, Precision-Recall (PR) curves, and Decision Curve Analysis (DCA). We also developed a web-based calculator for clinical deployment. To interpret model results, we utilized the SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) approach to assess feature importance.

Our findings indicate that the Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm achieved the best predictive performance, with an AUC of 0.75 (95% CI: 0.69–0.80) and a Brier score of 0.21 (95% CI: 0.17–0.28). The identified key features included creatinine, red blood cell count, neutrophil ratio, direct bilirubin, and monocyte count. We also developed a web-based tool for real-time risk stratification, providing a potential solution for clinical utility in resource-limited settings.

The design of functional materials necessitates the concurrent optimization of multiple interdependent properties to achieve optimal performance in various applications. In the context of boron-doped graphene (BDG), both the band gap and work function play critical roles, yet existing machine learning approaches often focus on predicting a single property using hand-crafted features, which hinders their applicability across different scenarios.

To address this limitation, we propose an adaptive edge-aware graph convolutional neural network with multi-task learning (AEGCNN-MTL) for simultaneous prediction of multiple material properties. Our approach leverages the strengths of graph neural networks and multi-task learning to capture complex relationships between interdependent properties. We evaluate AEGCNN-MTL on a dataset of 2613 DFT-computed BDG structures, demonstrating exceptional accuracy in predicting band gap (R² = 0.9905) and work function (R² = 0.9778). Notably, our framework outperforms single-task GNN baselines under identical training budgets, showcasing its potential for efficient multi-property prediction.

Furthermore, we transfer AEGCNN-MTL to the QM9 benchmark, where it exhibits competitive performance across 12 diverse quantum chemical properties, underscoring its strong generalization capability. These results highlight the promise of AEGCNN-MTL as a scalable and accurate tool for high-throughput material screening and data-driven discovery of multifunctional materials.

This study introduces an innovative method for generating a fingerprint for crystalline materials that reconciles efficiency in machine processing with human interpretability. The proposed approach comprises two key components: a structural representation capturing the crystal structure and a compositional component, referred to as Pettifor embedding, which characterizes the chemical composition.

The Pettifor embedding is constructed within a non-orthogonal space where each axis corresponds to a specific chemical element, with angles between axes quantifying similarity. The chemical composition is then defined by a point on the unit sphere in this space. Our results demonstrate that Pettifor embeddings outperform conventional elemental embeddings in compositional machine learning models.

Utilizing Pettifor embeddings as a distance metric and dimension reduction techniques, we develop a two-dimensional global map of thermodynamically stable crystalline compounds. Notably, these maps effectively separate material classes based on fundamental physical properties, despite their relatively simple construction. Our findings highlight the potential of Pettifor embeddings to facilitate a deeper understanding of structure-property relationships in crystalline materials and inform the development of more accurate machine learning models for materials science applications.

Ecosystems play a critical role in mitigating the impacts of climate change by absorbing significant portions of anthropogenic CO2 emissions. However, understanding ecosystem responses to climate variability requires accurate and consistent monitoring of carbon, water, and energy fluxes across diverse landscapes. Existing datasets often lack sufficient spatial resolution and consistency, particularly in fragmented regions such as UK agricultural areas. To address this gap, we present the Unified FLUXes (UFLUX) ensemble, a novel dataset of gross primary productivity, evapotranspiration, and sensible heat fluxes derived from eddy covariance data, satellite observations, and machine learning algorithms. UFLUX comprises approximately 60 ensemble members spanning multiple spatial and temporal scales, including global monthly averages at 0.25° resolution, European daily and biannual datasets at 0.25° and 100 m resolutions respectively, and UK daily datasets at 100 m resolution. Validation against eddy covariance measurements indicates that UFLUX captures over 80% of flux variability with low mean absolute errors, accurately reproducing climate responses and interannual patterns consistent with existing research. While uncertainties in net carbon flux remain, the UFLUX dataset holds significant promise for informing cross-scale climate policymaking and land management strategies aimed at achieving a carbon-neutral future.

The proliferation of multiplayer gaming has given rise to an escalating cat-and-mouse game between anticheat developers and cheaters, necessitating the development of robust anti-cheat mechanisms to ensure fair play. In response, anti-cheat systems have evolved significantly over the past few decades, incorporating advanced cryptographic techniques to counter various forms of cheating. However, conventional methods such as signature-based detection and memory scanning are increasingly ineffective against novel and adaptive threats. This study examines the limitations of these traditional approaches and explores modern AI-driven solutions, including computer vision-based aimbot detection and behavioral analysis using deep learning models. These cutting-edge techniques offer proactive and adaptive capabilities that can detect zero-day threats more effectively than conventional methods. Through a comparative analysis, this research identifies a critical gap in existing literature and proposes a novel hybrid security framework model that integrates the strengths of traditional anti-cheat methods with the dynamic detection power of AI-driven solutions. The proposed architecture leverages cryptographic technologies such as Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) for process isolation and Hierarchical Identity-Based Encryption (HIBE) for secure authentication, creating a resilient multilayered defense against cheating. Ultimately, this study provides a technical reference and architectural blueprint for designing the next generation of secure, cheat-resistant online gaming systems, with future research directions focused on integrating post-quantum cryptography and developing lightweight frameworks for mobile and VR platforms.

The optimization of sintered neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) magnets is a complex task, hindered by intricate structure-property relationships and limited data availability. To address this challenge, we have compiled the first comprehensive database for this system, encompassing 1994 industrial and academic samples. Our research aims to investigate the efficacy of active learning (AL) strategies in conjunction with classical and quantum-enhanced regressors. A thorough analysis of our curated dataset reveals distinct design heuristics employed by industrial and academic researchers, underscoring the importance of domain-aware considerations in materials science applications. Furthermore, we propose a novel methodological framework for integrating quantum kernel regression into an AL framework, utilizing bootstrapped ensembles to quantify uncertainty. Our experimental results demonstrate that the effectiveness of AL is contingent upon the chosen regressor model. Specifically, significant acceleration is observed when using Random Forest and Support Vector Regression models, whereas XGBoost exhibits diminished performance, and in some cases, AL proves detrimental compared to random sampling. These findings have far-reaching implications for the strategic application of machine learning in materials discovery, highlighting the need for careful model selection and domain-aware analysis in this field.

This study explores the application of machine learning techniques for modeling and predicting Energetic Intelligence, a construct that encompasses emotional, spiritual, and physical dimensions of human intelligence. Building on recent validation efforts, our research leverages data-driven approaches to investigate the feasibility of using machine learning algorithms in organizational contexts. We collected survey responses from individuals and employed a range of supervised learning methods to develop predictive models. Model performance was assessed through standard metrics, with optimized models achieving an R2 value of 0.73. Notably, variables such as Flow, Flourishing, and Emotional Vitality emerged as key predictors in model training, underscoring their relevance for Energetic Intelligence assessment. These findings suggest that machine learning can provide valuable insights into human potential and well-being when integrated with psychological theory. The study's outcomes have practical implications for the development of data-driven tools to support the assessment and cultivation of Energetic Intelligence in applied settings, highlighting the potential benefits of interdisciplinary collaboration between AI research and psychology.

The increasing reliance of cyber-physical systems on integrated circuits (ICs) has raised concerns about the vulnerability of these systems to malicious hardware attacks. Hardware Trojans, a type of stealthy threat, are malicious implants in circuitry that can remain dormant until triggered, causing functional disruptions or sensitive information leakage. Traditional IC validation methods often fail to detect these subtle anomalies due to their ability to mimic normal circuit behavior. To address this challenge, we propose a novel approach for detecting hardware Trojans through side-channel power analysis. Our method combines time-domain features with frequency-domain characteristics of power traces using a dual-domain feature extraction strategy. We developed an artificial intelligence (AI) based robust Trojan detection framework that integrates traditional machine learning models, including random forest, gradient boosting, naïve bayes, and deep learning models such as deep neural network, long short-term memory, and graph neural network. A stacked ensemble classifier is employed to leverage the strengths of these baseline models. Our results demonstrate that this ensemble-based detection method outperforms individual baseline models on the advanced encryption standard (AES)-Trojan benchmark, achieving a macro-averaged area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.987 without relying on a trusted reference IC for comparison. This approach enables direct anomaly detection from observable characteristics of untrusted chips, such as side-channel emissions, making it an attractive solution for ensuring the security and integrity of cyber-physical systems.

Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is a pervasive global health concern, necessitating the development of efficient and accurate prediction methods to inform timely interventions. Traditional diagnostic approaches often suffer from limitations such as prolonged processing times, substantial costs, and susceptibility to human error, underscoring the potential benefits of machine learning (ML) for enhanced risk assessment. However, ML-based CVD prediction is hindered by challenges including outliers, irrelevant features, class imbalance, and limited interpretability, which necessitate a comprehensive framework that addresses these issues.

To this end, we propose an integrated approach that leverages advanced data preprocessing techniques to detect and correct anomalies, feature selection methods to identify the most informative predictors, and ensemble learning strategies to mitigate class imbalance. Specifically, we employ Z-score-based outlier detection and XGBoost-driven feature selection to optimize model performance. Furthermore, we develop a suite of eight ML models, including Logistic Regression, KNN, SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost, and Gaussian Naive Bayes, which are rigorously evaluated using a range of metrics.

The results demonstrate that a voting ensemble combining the top-performing models (Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and AdaBoost) achieves exceptional accuracy (98.63%) and AUC-ROC (99.13%) on the Cleveland Heart Disease dataset, significantly outperforming individual models. Importantly, SHAP and LIME analyses provide actionable insights into model predictions, facilitating informed clinical decision-making and patient risk stratification. This work contributes a robust, interpretable, and high-performing ML framework for early CVD detection, with potential applications in clinical practice.

Predictive Modeling for Timely Identification of Febrile Patients Requiring Hospitalization in Emergency Departments

The timely identification of febrile patients necessitating hospitalization remains a pressing concern within Emergency Departments (EDs). Delayed intervention can lead to increased mortality, underscoring the need for effective predictive models. This study presents an innovative approach utilizing pre-trained language models to forecast ED disposition decisions based on triage information from 25,405 febrile patient visits. A predictive model was developed and validated using vital signs and self-reported symptoms to identify patients requiring hospitalization or readmission within a 72-hour window. The proposed methodology involves transforming numerical vital signs into narrative text, enabling the integration of clinical narratives through a novel architecture. The results demonstrate superior performance compared to traditional machine learning methods and biomedical language models, with area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) values reaching 0.9226, area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) at 0.8767, and macro F₁-score of 0.8446. Notably, demographic patterns emerged, indicating a higher likelihood of hospitalization among elderly patients, males, and those arriving by ambulance. This predictive model addresses critical ED challenges by facilitating early patient identification, reducing inappropriate discharges, and optimizing resource allocation, ultimately enhancing patient outcomes and ED efficiency.

This study investigates the interplay between genetic predisposition, lifestyle, and all-cause mortality in the context of obesity. Despite the well-documented influence of genetics on body weight, the complex relationships among genetic susceptibility to obesity, lifestyle choices, and increased risk of mortality remain poorly understood. To address this knowledge gap, we developed and validated a machine learning model that integrates genetic data from two large cohorts: 482,700 UK Biobank participants and 8,607 Nanfang Hospital participants. The model generates an obesity-related polygenic risk score (OPRS), which enables the evaluation of associations between genetic risk of obesity, lifestyle factors, and all-cause mortality. Our results indicate that a high OPRS is significantly correlated with increased all-cause mortality, particularly among individuals with normal weight or overweight status. Notably, adherence to four healthy lifestyle factors among individuals with high genetic risk of obesity was found to reduce the risk of all-cause mortality by 59% compared to those without such lifestyle modifications. These findings highlight the critical role of genetics in determining obesity-related health outcomes and underscore the importance of a healthy lifestyle in mitigating the adverse effects of genetic predisposition on mortality risk.

In post-mastectomy reconstruction, precise estimation of breast volume and weight is crucial for optimal outcomes. However, existing methods often involve significant costs or complexity. To address these limitations, we developed a machine learning framework that utilizes demographic and anthropometric data to predict breast volume and weight. Our study involved collecting data from 199 patients between 2021 and 2023, which underwent rigorous pre-processing, feature selection, model training, and performance evaluation. We employed three distinct feature selection techniques: domain expert knowledge, Spearman's rank correlation, and the Boruta algorithm. Each feature set was used to train a range of models, including linear regression, random forest regression, and support vector regression. Model performance was assessed using metrics such as R2 values and Pearson's correlation coefficient. Notably, significant correlations were observed between breast volume or weight and patient characteristics like BMI, breast cup size, ptosis severity, and anthropometric measurements. The optimal model, combining domain-expert and statistically selected features via linear regression, achieved R2 values of 81.8% for breast volume and 72% for breast weight. These findings demonstrate that integrating machine learning with demographic and anthropometric data yields a highly accurate, interpretable, and accessible method for preoperative breast assessment. In contrast to conventional imaging or mathematical models, this approach eliminates costs related to equipment, relies on routinely collected clinical data, reduces the need for specialized training, and enables seamless integration into existing clinical workflows. By overcoming traditional methods' limitations, our proposed model provides a practical, efficient, and cost-effective solution for clinical practice.

The phenomenon of fracture has far-reaching implications across various disciplines, encompassing infrastructure resilience, aerospace durability, reservoir productivity, and seismic event analysis. To address the need for accurate material failure prediction, this study presents a diverse dataset of simulated fracture evolution and material degradation generated using two numerical solvers: the phase-field method and the combined finite-discrete element method (FDEM). These solvers exhibit distinct formulations, physical fidelity levels, and computational efficiency characteristics.

The dataset comprises five materials: PBX, anisotropic shale, tungsten, aluminum, and steel. For each material, extensive phase-field simulations were conducted, totaling 400,000 cases: 200,000 under uniaxial tension and 200,000 under biaxial tension. In contrast, the computationally intensive FDEM simulations consisted of 90,000 cases, evenly distributed among PBX, shale, and tungsten under uniaxial loading conditions. All simulations initiated with randomly generated initial fracture patterns, capturing temporal data on fracture propagation dynamics.

This comprehensive dataset is designed to facilitate the development of foundational or surrogate machine learning approaches for predicting material failure. By providing a robust foundation for future research, this study aims to advance innovation in areas where accurate material failure prediction is critical. While no specific machine learning models are introduced here, the presented dataset serves as a crucial stepping stone for advancing predictive capabilities in these domains.

Developmental dysgraphia is a complex neurological disorder that significantly impairs children's writing abilities, making accurate diagnosis particularly challenging due to its heterogeneous symptoms and frequent co-occurrence with other disorders. Recent research has explored machine learning approaches for dysgraphia diagnosis using either offline or online handwriting data, but these studies have typically analyzed the two modalities separately, neglecting the potential benefits of integrating them. To address this limitation, we propose a novel multimodal machine learning approach that combines both online and offline handwriting data to improve diagnostic accuracy. A unique dataset was created by transforming an existing online handwriting dataset into its offline counterpart, allowing for a comprehensive analysis of word types, including simple words, pseudowords, and difficult words. We employed support vector machines (SVM) and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) classifiers on both online and offline features, as well as multimodal feature fusion and soft-voted ensemble methods. Additionally, we introduced an innovative ensemble with conditional feature fusion, which selectively integrates feature-level fusion when confidence scores fall below a certain threshold. Our proposed approach achieved an impressive accuracy of 88.8%, outperforming single-modality SVMs by 12-14 percentage points and existing methods by 8-9 percentage points. This methodology offers significant contributions to the preliminary screening of handwriting difficulties through multimodal analysis of word/pseudoword data, providing a promising initial assessment tool that supports dysgraphia diagnosis. The findings highlight the potential of multimodal learning in enhancing dysgraphia assessment, paving the way for more accessible and effective diagnostic tools.

The development of effective recommender systems has been hindered by their reliance on a single type of user-item interaction, such as purchases or ratings. In contrast, real-world users exhibit diverse behaviors, including clicks and cart additions, which provide a more nuanced understanding of their preferences. To capitalize on these varied interactions, multi-behavior recommender systems have emerged as a promising approach, leveraging multiple types of user behavior to enhance recommendation accuracy.

This study aims to provide an in-depth examination of the current state of multi-behavior recommender systems, focusing on three critical components: data modeling, encoding, and training. Through a systematic analysis, we categorize existing approaches based on their similarities and differences across these steps, highlighting both the strengths and limitations of each method. Our investigation also explores potential avenues for advancing this field, including novel techniques for representing multi-behavior interactions and optimizing machine learning models.

By reviewing the current landscape of multi-behavior recommender systems and identifying areas for improvement, our research seeks to inform the development of more effective recommendation algorithms that can capture the complexity of real-world user behavior.

The development of coagulation factor X (FXa) inhibitors has been a significant area of research in recent years, with a focus on creating safer and more selective therapeutic agents. Despite the progress made, there remains a pressing need for improved predictive models that can accurately identify potential FXa inhibitors from large datasets of small molecules. This study addresses this challenge by developing an interpretable machine learning-based quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) framework to predict both the inhibitory potency and activity class of compounds targeting FXa. A comprehensive dataset of 6400 structurally curated compounds was compiled from ChEMBL, standardized, and encoded using a set of 391 non-redundant Mordred descriptors. Benchmarking experiments revealed that ExtraTreesRegressor and XGBoostClassifier were the most robust models for regression and classification tasks, respectively. The proposed QSAR pipeline achieved impressive performance metrics, with an R2 value of 0.760 and RMSE of 0.831 in regression, as well as accuracy, balanced precision, recall, and ROC-AUC values of 0.91, 0.92, 0.90, and 0.962 in classification. SHAP analysis provided valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms driving FXa inhibitory potency, highlighting the importance of electrostatic, topological, and polar surface descriptors. The applicability domain assessment confirmed that the model's predictions were reliable for both training and test sets. Overall, this study presents a robust and interpretable QSAR framework that offers a practical computational tool for virtual screening and rational design of novel FXa inhibitors.

This study examines the graph recitation and generation capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), with implications for their emergent properties and potential applications in network science. We investigate two tasks: first, LLMs' ability to accurately recall well-known graphs from literature, such as the Karate club or Graph atlas; second, their capacity to generate Erdős–Rényi random graphs. To assess errors, we propose a metric focusing on hallucination – incorrect information presented as factual. Our results indicate that graph hallucinations can serve as a distinguishing feature among LLMs, with higher amplitudes correlating with superior performance in the recitation task. Notably, this correlation is observed even when considering the Hallucination Leaderboard, which ranks models based on 10,000 times more prompts than our study. In contrast, LLMs exhibit surprisingly good and reproducible results in generating Erdős–Rényi random graphs. These findings provide a starting point for further investigation into LLMs' emergent capabilities and offer a challenging benchmark for their development. The intersection of network science and machine learning is highlighted through this research, underscoring the potential for LLMs to contribute meaningfully to both fields.

The subjective nature of pain presents significant challenges for accurate assessment in clinical practice, with traditional methods relying heavily on self-reported measures that can be influenced by individual differences in communication skills and emotional expression. Recent research has highlighted the potential of physiological signals to provide an objective measure of pain, capitalizing on the well-documented impact of pain on the Autonomic Nervous System. This study contributes to this growing area of interest by examining the application of machine learning techniques to physiological signal analysis for pain assessment.

A comprehensive review of the literature was conducted, focusing on studies published between 2014 and 2024 that explored the use of machine learning methods for pain biomarker development. The findings are organized around two key themes: first, an examination of the variability in signal feature behavior across different pain types, and second, a critical evaluation of current state-of-the-art models developed using classical machine learning and deep learning approaches.

The results demonstrate the potential of machine learning-based approaches to provide accurate and objective assessments of pain, highlighting areas for future research and development. The study's findings have significant implications for the advancement of pain assessment techniques in clinical practice, with potential applications in both acute and chronic pain management settings.

The widespread adoption of machine learning algorithms has underscored their susceptibility to adversarial attacks, which can be perpetrated by manipulating input data with subtle yet effective tampering techniques. As quantum computing capabilities continue to advance, the prospect of harnessing quantum machine learning (QML) algorithms has sparked interest in their potential vulnerabilities to such attacks. This study investigates the inherent properties of QML algorithms that may confer resistance against adversarial manipulation, examining whether quantum effects can provide a safeguard against classically-armed adversaries. By drawing on tools from many-body physics, we identify the quantum mechanisms underlying this protection and demonstrate its efficacy in various scenarios. Our results reveal that quantum classifiers exhibit robustness against specific types of attacks: they are impervious to weak perturbations within the trained distribution, resilient to local attacks if they fail to induce sufficient data scrambling, and show evidence of resistance against universal adversarial attacks when sufficiently chaotic. Numerical simulations corroborate our analytical findings, underscoring the practical applicability of QML in mitigating adversarial vulnerabilities. This research contributes a theoretical foundation for recent empirical evidence suggesting quantum advantages in detecting adversarial robustness, thereby offering a novel pathway to leveraging QML's potential benefits beyond traditional performance enhancements.

The accurate assessment of railway infrastructure is crucial for ensuring operational safety and efficiency. However, the development of data-driven methods for this purpose has been hindered by the lack of large-scale, finely annotated 3D datasets. To address this limitation, we present a novel dataset, SemanticRail3D-V2, which builds upon our previously introduced SemanticRail3D dataset. The latter comprises 438 high-resolution point clouds (approximately 2.8 billion points) in 200 m segments, annotated with 12 semantic classes and instance labels, along with per-point intensity information. In this updated version, we introduce a machine learning-ready preprocessing pipeline that aligns and sections raw scans into uniform blocks. Furthermore, we propose an evaluation protocol that combines metric-based anomaly detection with probabilistic validity analysis of class distributions and spatial relationships. The dataset has been extensively reviewed by railway domain experts to ensure its quality and accuracy, resulting in five training shards, a validation set, and a held-out test set. This rigorously curated benchmark offers a rich source of annotated data for semantic and instance segmentation research in railway environments, with applications in condition monitoring and asset management.

Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) have been increasingly adopted in healthcare settings to enhance medical decision-making and improve patient outcomes. However, developing rules for these systems can be a time-consuming and costly process due to the complexities involved in capturing and defining the rules through multiple iterations between clinicians and developers. This study investigates the potential of large language models (LLMs) and large reasoning models (LRMs) in generating triaging rule sets for CDSS.

To evaluate their effectiveness, we prompted various LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Gemini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) and LRMs (GPT-o1-mini, Grok-4, Claude 4 Sonnet) using alternative prompting techniques and compared the generated rule sets against a clinical rule set from our Pandemic Intervention Monitoring System (PiMS), a triaging CDSS built in collaboration with clinicians to monitor COVID-19 positive patients. The performance of LLMs was found to be limited when generating rule sets, particularly when not specifying variables from the PiMS rule set. However, by including these variables in the prompts, LLMs achieved an average accuracy between 31.62% and 70.71%. In contrast, LRMs demonstrated improved effectiveness, with average accuracies ranging from 31.62% to 81.70%.

Our findings suggest that while LLMs are limited in emulating clinical rule sets due to their simplicity and lack of complex reasoning, they can still generate a feasible initial rule set for CDSS, potentially reducing the time invested by clinicians and developers in refinement. Furthermore, LRMs showed promise in improving effectiveness, but further research is needed to explore their integration with decision trees to enhance performance.

The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in epidemic response has garnered significant attention in recent years due to its potential to predict disease patterns, control outbreaks, and optimize resource allocation. This study focuses on the application of AI in Lassa fever management, a highly infectious and deadly viral hemorrhagic fever prevalent in West Africa. The analysis begins by examining the epidemiological characteristics of Lassa fever, including transmission dynamics and clinical manifestations, which informs the development of AI-driven approaches. Key AI technologies such as machine learning algorithms, deep learning models, and natural language processing techniques are evaluated for their effectiveness in surveillance, diagnostics, and treatment innovation. Notably, predictive modeling has emerged as a promising strategy for outbreak identification and early warning systems. However, the adoption of AI in epidemic contexts is hindered by several challenges, including data scarcity, computational resource constraints, and integration difficulties with existing healthcare infrastructure. This study aims to address these limitations by providing an in-depth examination of AI-driven techniques, recent advancements, and practical implementation strategies tailored to Lassa fever control. By extrapolating insights from this case study, the research seeks to inform stakeholders, policymakers, and healthcare professionals on the strategic deployment of AI to enhance public health preparedness and epidemic management capabilities.

This study contributes to the advancement of handwritten character recognition by developing a novel dataset designed for rigorous evaluation of machine learning models. Building upon existing NIST Databases, our dataset offers a unique opportunity for researchers to re-examine and refine their approaches in this domain. We provide an overview of prominent publicly available machine learning architectures trained on the EMNIST-letters dataset, highlighting key characteristics and performance metrics. Notably, we identify limitations in current evaluation practices, including the reliance on cross-validation settings that may not accurately reflect real-world scenarios. To address these concerns, our dataset features new, independently generated data that is unaffiliated with the NIST database authors. This enables a more comprehensive assessment of model robustness and generalizability. The dataset and accompanying source codes are made publicly available through the Gdansk Tech University repository Most Wiedzy. Our work aims to foster a deeper understanding of machine learning models in handwritten character recognition, ultimately contributing to improved performance and reliability in this critical application area.

This study investigates the effectiveness of artificial intelligence (AI) applied to electrocardiography (ECG) for mortality prediction by systematically evaluating model design choices across three large medical center cohorts. Our analysis encompasses a comprehensive evaluation of various neural architectures, including convolutional neural networks and transformers, in comparison with demographic-only and gradient boosting baselines. Three datasets from diverse populations were utilized: Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center (MIMIC-IV), Telehealth Network of Minas Gerais (Code-15), and Boston Children's Hospital (BCH). The results demonstrate that deep survival methods consistently outperform horizon-based classification, with the inclusion of demographic covariates such as age and sex significantly improving predictive performance. Notably, classifier-Cox models exhibit site-dependent sensitivity to horizon choice, highlighting the need for site-specific calibration. Furthermore, our multi-site experiment reveals that cross-cohort training can substantially improve performance on those cohorts compared to cohort-specific training. These findings underscore the importance of model type, demographic features, and training data diversity in developing AI-ECG models that can be reliably applied across populations.

In recent years, mental health screening has become increasingly essential in clinical settings due to rising demand. However, traditional assessment methods often involve lengthy and time-consuming procedures that can be burdensome for patients. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach utilizing multimodal machine learning to efficiently screen multiple conditions from a single video response. Our model integrates MPNet for text analysis with HuBERT for voice prosody processing, trained on a dataset comprising 2,420 participants. The results demonstrate that our method can significantly reduce assessment time by 64.6% (78.4 seconds vs 221.7 seconds) while accurately screening for depression, anxiety, and trauma. Notably, only a small proportion of participants (1.4%) expressed reluctance to use video-based screening. Furthermore, the model exhibits strong performance across various demographic groups, including age, gender, and race/ethnicity, underscoring its feasibility as an efficient multi-condition assessment tool in clinical settings.
